zh-tw.title: 端午節笑話大全
zh-tw.description: 粽子諧音梗、屈原哲學、龍舟冷知識——端午節最應景的搞笑語錄與節日幽默

en.title: Dragon Boat Festival Jokes
en.description: Zongzi puns, Qu Yuan humor, and dragon boat wisecracks to celebrate the season in style

items.sourceNote: 粽 homophone pun format from 端午祝福語 compilations; rewritten with original dialogue structure

zh-tw.content:
「你知道端午節我最想說什麼嗎？」
「什麼？」
「粽是有你，我的生活才有滋有味。」

對方沉默了三秒。

「你這個人怎麼這麼粽蛋。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 節日限定甜蜜對話，「粽是有你」=「總是有你」，「粽蛋」=「笨蛋」——適合傳給另一半或暗戀的人，笑中帶甜，對方如果沒有笑可能是你太認真了
variations: 單句版：「端午節快樂，粽是有你才幸福。」——適合傳給不熟的朋友，簡單安全不尷尬

en.content:
"Know what I want to say most this Dragon Boat Festival?"
"What?"
"Zong-ways and always, life is better with you in it."

Three seconds of silence.

"You're such a little dumpling."
en.editorNote: Sweet and punny holiday exchange — 'zong-ways' echoes 'always' (粽/zong sounds like 總/always in Chinese). Works as a caption for festival couple photos too
variations: Short version: "Happy Dragon Boat Festival — zong-ways thinking of you."

items.sourceNote: 一見鍾情 → 一見粽情 classic pun; original joke dialogue rewritten

zh-tw.content:
阿志拿起第一顆粽子，二話不說就咬了下去。

旁邊的阿嬤說：「你怎麼不先問問是甜的還是鹹的？」

阿志：「我這個人就是一見粽情，哪管什麼口味。」

阿嬤：「……你不是也這樣娶了你老婆嗎？」

阿志沉默了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 「一見粽情」=「一見鍾情」，笑點在阿嬤神補刀——適合傳到家族群組，尤其阿嬤本人在線上的時候效果最好
variations: 短版：「我對粽子向來是一見粽情，從不先問口味的。」

en.content:
Zhi grabbed the first zongzi and bit in without checking.

His grandma: "You didn't even ask if it's sweet or savory?"

Zhi: "I'm a love-at-first-bite kind of person."

Grandma: "Isn't that how you married your wife too?"

Zhi went quiet.
en.editorNote: Classic grandma counter-attack. Works great in family group chats — the punchline lands harder when everyone knows the couple in question
variations: Short version: "I'm strictly a love-at-first-bite zongzi person. I don't do flavor research first."

items.sourceNote: 粽望所歸 (眾望所歸) pun from 端午賀詞 pool; rewritten as family scene

zh-tw.content:
每年端午節，阿嬤包的粽子都是全家的期待。

阿嬤說：「哎呀，不過就是幾顆粽子嘛。」

全家：「阿嬤，您這才叫粽望所歸。」

阿嬤笑得合不攏嘴，然後又多包了二十顆。
zh-tw.editorNote: 「粽望所歸」=「眾望所歸」——適合配上阿嬤包粽子的照片傳到家族群組，或端午節感謝長輩的暖心文案
variations: 職場版：「這個專案全靠你了，你才是我們團隊的粽望所歸。」——傳給那個在關鍵時刻撐起全場的同事

en.content:
Every Dragon Boat Festival, Grandma's zongzi is what the whole family waits for.

Grandma: "It's just a few rice dumplings, don't make a fuss."

The whole family: "Grandma, you're the MVP of this holiday."

Grandma smiled and immediately made twenty more.
en.editorNote: Warm family humor for festival photo captions — works best paired with a photo of grandma wrapping zongzi. The MVP setup gets universal family recognition
variations: Workplace version: "This project would fall apart without you. You're the real MVP of this team."

items.sourceNote: 屈原 + 股市 cross-joke concept from Dcard 端午節笑話 thread; rewritten with original structure

zh-tw.content:
端午節前夕，股市崩盤。

同事阿強看著手機，深吸一口氣說：

「我現在終於理解屈原了。」

「是啊，報國無門，心灰意冷——」

「不是，是看著數字一直往下，人就想往水裡跑。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給有在玩股票的朋友，尤其在端午節前後股市震盪時特別應景——笑完之後記得把他拉回來，不要讓他真的去找屈原
variations: 安慰版：「屈原是含冤投江，你只是小賠，完全不一樣，還有翻身機會的。」

en.content:
The day before Dragon Boat Festival, the market tanked.

Colleague Qiang stared at his phone and took a deep breath:

"I finally understand Qu Yuan."

"Yeah, a man abandoned by his kingdom, broken-hearted—"

"No. I mean watching the numbers keep dropping makes a person want to run toward the water."
en.editorNote: Send to that friend who's been watching their portfolio this week. Works especially well right before a long weekend when markets close — gallows humor at its most relatable
variations: Reassurance version: "Qu Yuan jumped over a fallen kingdom. You lost 8%. Completely different situation. There's still a comeback."

items.sourceNote: 南粽北粽 debate format widely circulated; original 3-party dialogue rewritten with 屈原 resolution

zh-tw.content:
北粽派：「粽子就要甜的！紅豆、花生才正統！」

南粽派：「沒包肉的哪叫粽子，那叫甜湯包！」

兩派爭得不可開交。

角落裡的阿公緩緩說：

「屈原當年投江，是因為楚國滅了，不是因為你們這樣吵。」

大家都沉默了。

然後同時又說：「所以甜的才對！」/「所以鹹的才對！」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳到家族群組，讓每年必吵的口味大戰有個完美收尾——屈原神補刀之後大家再繼續吵，反而更歡樂
variations: 簡版：「南北粽之爭幾千年沒有答案，就連屈原也沒說他喜歡哪種。」

en.content:
Team Sweet Zongzi: "Real zongzi is sweet — red bean and peanut, the classic!"

Team Savory Zongzi: "No meat? That's just a sweet rice ball. Not a zongzi!"

The debate raged on.

Grandpa in the corner finally said:
"Qu Yuan jumped into a river because his kingdom fell. Not because of this argument."

Everyone went quiet.

Then simultaneously: "So sweet ones are right!" / "So savory ones are right!"
en.editorNote: The perfect festival group chat entry — the Qu Yuan line temporarily stops the fight, and then everyone starts again, which is somehow even funnier
variations: Neutral version: "The sweet vs. savory debate has no winner. Even Qu Yuan never revealed his preference."

items.sourceNote: Dragon boat leak joke from English joke site; rewritten with new punchline structure

zh-tw.content:
問：龍舟隊為什麼不適合保守秘密？

答：因為他們的船很容易「漏」出去。

補充：而且還有二十個人一起喊節奏，沒有任何事可以悄悄進行。
zh-tw.editorNote: 「漏」雙關：船漏水 + 消息外漏——適合傳給喜歡英文冷笑話風格的朋友，或在有龍舟活動的端午聚會上說
variations: 進階版：「為什麼龍舟隊的人不能當臥底？因為他們的工作就是讓所有人都聽到他們。」

en.content:
Why can't dragon boat teams keep secrets?

Because their boats always spring a leak.

Also: there are twenty people chanting in rhythm. Nothing about this operation is discreet.
en.editorNote: Clean, punchy bilingual joke — perfect for festival IG stories or sending to friends who appreciate dry English humor. The add-on line makes it land harder
variations: Extended: "Why can't dragon boat racers be undercover agents? Because their whole job is to be heard from across the water."

items.sourceNote: 粽情投入 (全情投入) pun from holiday greeting compilations; rewritten with workplace setting

zh-tw.content:
公司端午節團建活動：包粽子比賽。

主管大力鼓勵：「今天大家要粽情投入！」

員工阿豪完全沒有廚藝，包出來的粽子像足球，像枕頭，還有一個不知道是什麼形狀。

最後全部在鍋裡散開。

主管：「……你確實投入了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 「粽情投入」=「全情投入」——傳給在公司活動上最認真但結果一塌糊塗的同事，他看完應該又哭又笑；也適合傳給那個喜歡喊口號的主管
variations: 正向版：「包粽子本來就不是人人都會，但粽情投入的態度是最重要的。」

en.content:
Company Dragon Boat Festival team-building: zongzi-making competition.

Manager: "Today, everyone gives it their all!"

Employee Hao had zero cooking skills. His zongzi came out shaped like a football, a pillow, and something unidentifiable.

All of them unraveled in the pot.

Manager: "...You certainly gave it everything."
en.editorNote: For the teammate who brings 110% effort to everything and still somehow ends up with a disaster — they'll laugh hardest at themselves. Also perfect for the manager who loves motivational slogans
variations: Positive spin: "Making zongzi isn't for everyone, but the spirit of going all-in is what counts."

items.sourceNote: Dumpling/zongzi wrapping metaphor concept; original philosophical parallel rewritten

zh-tw.content:
人生跟粽子很像：

外面一層一層的葉子，是你為了保護自己包起來的殼。

裡面真正的料，才是那個真實的你。

而且——包得越緊，蒸出來越香。

所以下次有人說你把自己包得太緊，
你就說：「那是因為我還沒被蒸熟。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 端午節限定哲理語錄，結尾的反轉讓它從勵志變成廢話文學風格——適合發朋友圈或IG，引發朋友回覆「什麼鬼」
variations: 純勵志版：「人生就像粽子：包得越緊，越能保住自己最好的那部分。」

en.content:
Life is a lot like a zongzi:

The layers of leaves wrapped around it are the protective shell you build for yourself.

The real filling inside is who you actually are.

And the tighter it's wrapped, the better it steams.

So next time someone tells you that you're too guarded,
just say: "I haven't been fully cooked yet."
en.editorNote: Starts as a motivational metaphor and ends as absurdist self-defense logic — perfect as a festival caption that makes your followers do a double-take
variations: Straight version: "Like a zongzi: the tighter you're wrapped, the better you hold together under pressure."

items.sourceNote: 屈原 + 現代社群媒體 parallel; original concept rewritten with contemporary platform details

zh-tw.content:
如果屈原活在現代，他大概不會投江。

他會開帳號，寫長文控訴楚王的昏庸無能。
留言區立刻炸鍋，清一色「這個我看不下去了」。

文章被轉爆，屈原成為年度最佳政論作家。

楚王打開手機，看到自己的名字上了熱搜，
叫來侍從：「幫我也開個帳號。」

屈原：「……好的，我來幫你按封鎖。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給喜歡歷史梗或社群媒體幽默的朋友，結尾的神反轉讓人笑完之後回味一下——屈原到現代，依然輸不了
variations: 簡版：「屈原活在現代：寫長文控訴，留言全是『這個我看不下去了』，熱搜第一。」

en.content:
If Qu Yuan lived today, he probably wouldn't jump in a river.

He'd open an account, write a long thread exposing the king's failures.
The comments would explode: "I can't stay silent about this."

The post goes viral. Qu Yuan becomes the top political commentator of the year.

The king opens his phone, sees his name trending,
and calls his assistant: "Set me up an account too."

Qu Yuan: "...Already got you blocked."
en.editorNote: For history buffs and social media addicts — the ending twist where Qu Yuan wins by blocking the king is a crowd-pleaser in any group chat
variations: Short version: "Qu Yuan today: one viral thread, all comments say 'I can't stay quiet about this,' trending number one."

items.sourceNote: 粽是如此 (總是如此) pun with grandma dialogue; original scene written for this topic

zh-tw.content:
每年端午，阿嬤說的第一句話粽是如此：
「今年包的粽子，比去年還好吃。」

孫子：「阿嬤，妳去年也說一樣的話。」

阿嬤：「粽是如此，好吃的東西就是每年都更好吃。」

孫子：「這個邏輯……有點粽蛋。」

阿嬤：「叫你吃就對了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 「粽是如此」=「總是如此」——家庭溫馨場景，笑點在阿嬤的邏輯無懈可擊，適合配上阿嬤包粽子的照片傳家族群組
variations: 單句版：「阿嬤說今年的粽子比去年好吃——她粽是如此說。」

en.content:
Every Dragon Boat Festival, the first thing Grandma says is always the same:
"This year's zongzi is even better than last year's."

Grandchild: "Grandma, you say that every single year."

Grandma: "Because it's true every single year. Good food keeps getting better."

Grandchild: "That's... not really how it works."

Grandma: "Just eat it."
en.editorNote: The grandma who has no interest in logic, only in feeding you — universally relatable. Pair with a family festival photo for maximum engagement
variations: Short version: "Grandma's annual verdict: 'This year's zongzi is the best yet.' Same as last year. Same as every year."

items.sourceNote: 屈原 vs. 現代上班族 emotional parallel concept; original dialogue rewritten

zh-tw.content:
屈原：「我是因為被小人陷害、眼見山河破碎，才含冤投江的。」

現代上班族：「我也懂，就是那種努力了半天卻沒人看見，還被主管扣了一半的功勞——」

屈原：「……我們的情況完全不一樣。」

上班族：「但心情是一樣的。」

屈原沉默了一下：「行吧，我請你吃粽子。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個這週心情不好的同事，結尾屈原請吃粽子的神轉折讓整個畫面溫馨起來——黑色幽默加甜
variations: 簡版：「屈原不一定理解你的工作，但那個走到河邊的心情，他懂。」

en.content:
Qu Yuan: "I threw myself in the river because I was betrayed, watched my kingdom crumble, and died carrying a false name."

Modern office worker: "I get it — you work your hardest and no one notices, and the manager takes half the credit—"

Qu Yuan: "...Our situations are completely different."

Office worker: "The feeling is the same though."

Qu Yuan paused.

"Fine. I'll buy you a zongzi."
en.editorNote: Send to that coworker who's been having a rough week — the Qu Yuan buying zongzi ending is the unexpected warmth that saves it from being purely dark
variations: Short version: "Qu Yuan may not understand your workload, but the walk to the river? He gets it."

items.sourceNote: Dragon boat drummer role concept from English pun sites; original Q&A structure rewritten

zh-tw.content:
問：龍舟比賽中最難搞的人是誰？

答：打鼓的。

為什麼？

因為他坐在最前面，什麼都不用划，
但全船的節奏都得聽他的。

換句話說：他就是那個什麼都不做，
卻把別人的命運握在手裡的那個。

問：這聽起來像不像你的老闆？
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給同事，尤其是對主管有怨念的時候，一句「他就是那個打鼓的」可以在辦公室流行起來——但記得不要被主管看到
variations: 正向版：「打鼓的人設定節奏，讓整艘船前進——有時候領導就是這樣，你不划船，但你決定快慢。」

en.content:
Who's the hardest person to deal with in a dragon boat race?

The drummer.

Why?

They sit at the front and do no rowing.
But everyone on the boat has to follow their rhythm.

In other words: the one who does nothing
but holds the fate of everyone else in their hands.

Does that remind you of anyone at work?
en.editorNote: Send to your coworkers and watch as at least one person immediately replies with their manager's name — universally relatable festival humor
variations: Positive spin: "The drummer sets the pace that moves the whole boat forward. Leadership is knowing when to beat faster and when to hold steady."

items.sourceNote: 粽子 gift card pun concept; 粽要 (重要) and 粽蛋 (笨蛋) puns combined in card exchange scene

zh-tw.content:
端午節想送禮，挑了一盒精緻粽子，附上一張手寫卡：

「你是我心中最粽要的人。」

對方拆開，盯著卡片看了三秒，

然後回傳一張照片——他把「粽要」圈了起來，
旁邊寫：「你這個粽蛋，我好喜歡你。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 「粽要」=「重要」，「粽蛋」=「笨蛋」——適合傳給幽默感好的另一半或暗戀對象，送粽子加手寫卡這個組合本身就已經很土味了，成功率極高
variations: 單人版：「自己寫給自己：你是自己心中最粽要的人，記得好好照顧自己。」

en.content:
Needed a gift for Dragon Boat Festival.
Bought a nice box of zongzi, added a handwritten card:

"You're the most important person in my world."

Three seconds after they opened it, they sent back a photo —
they'd circled the "important" part
and written next to it: "You absolute dumpling. I like you so much."
en.editorNote: Zongzi as a romantic gift is already peak cheesy — the handwritten card pushes it into territory that's so earnest it wraps back around to charming. Works best on someone with a good sense of humor
variations: Self-care version: "To yourself this festival: you're the most important person in your own story. Don't forget it."

items.sourceNote: 包粽子失敗 scenario; original comedic situation written for beginner cook character

zh-tw.content:
第一次包粽子，阿如信心滿滿地坐下來。

二十分鐘後，面前有六個「作品」：
一個像足球，一個像枕頭，
一個像問號，一個像問號的問號，
還有一個她自己也不確定。

最後的那顆，勉強可以稱為粽子。

阿嬤走過來看了看：「你這是在包粽子，還是在重新詮釋抽象藝術？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給第一次嘗試包粽子的朋友，或在端午節家庭活動後拍下自己的「作品」傳群組——自嘲型內容最容易讓大家一起笑，而不是被笑
variations: 自嘲版：「我包的粽子不是粽子，是我對傳統文化的現代演繹。」

en.content:
First time making zongzi. A-Ru sat down with total confidence.

Twenty minutes later, she had six "creations":
One looked like a football. One like a pillow.
One like a question mark. One like a question mark's question mark.
And one she couldn't classify.

The last one barely qualified as zongzi.

Grandma walked over, looked at the spread, and said:
"Are you making zongzi or exploring abstract sculpture?"
en.editorNote: Perfect to send after attempting zongzi for the first time — pair with a photo of your results for maximum effect. Self-deprecating content always lands in group chats
variations: Self-defense: "These aren't failed zongzi. They're my modern interpretation of an ancient tradition."

items.sourceNote: 包粽萬象 (包羅萬象) and 粽是如此 puns; rewritten as holiday greeting card format

zh-tw.content:
端午節快樂！

祝你：
事業粽是順心 🎋
感情一見粽情 💚
財運包粽萬象 🎁
身體粽情投入 💪

然後吃太多粽子，撐得剛剛好。
zh-tw.editorNote: 節日祝福語集合版，把四個常見祝福詞全部換成粽子諧音梗——適合直接複製傳給親朋好友，省時省力又有誠意（而且讓對方感覺你很用心）
variations: 簡化版：「端午節快樂，願你粽是如意，一見粽情，包粽萬象。」

en.content:
Happy Dragon Boat Festival! 🐉

Wishing you:
Career success — always zong-going strong 🎋
Love life — love at first bite 💚
Wealth — wrapped in abundance 🎁
Health — all-in and thriving 💪

And may you eat exactly one too many zongzi.
en.editorNote: A ready-to-copy festival greeting with just enough zongzi wordplay to get a smile — the final line about eating one too many is the touch that makes it feel human instead of generic
variations: Short version: "Happy Dragon Boat Festival — wishing you love at first bite and a wallet as stuffed as a zongzi."

items.sourceNote: Dragon boat training / endurance concept; original joke about festival fatigue rewritten

zh-tw.content:
健身教練：「你今年的運動目標呢？」

阿豪：「端午節跟著朋友去看龍舟，
從早站到晚，走了三萬步，
還搬了一箱粽子。」

教練：「然後呢？」

阿豪：「然後回家吃了六顆粽子，
把今天全部的熱量補了回來。」

教練：「……我還是先說下次見。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個在端午節努力運動又立刻吃回來的朋友——這是節日限定的人類行為，教練見了也只能苦笑
variations: 自嘲版：「端午節走了三萬步，最後敗給了一鍋現包的熱粽子。這不叫失敗，這叫文化體驗。」

en.content:
Fitness coach: "What's your exercise goal this year?"

Hao: "At the Dragon Boat Festival I stood all day watching races,
walked 30,000 steps, and carried a crate of zongzi home."

Coach: "And then?"

Hao: "And then I ate six zongzi and took back every calorie I'd burned."

Coach: "...I'll see you next time, I guess."
en.editorNote: For the friend who exercised impressively at the festival and immediately undid it all with zongzi — this is a universal Dragon Boat Festival experience
variations: Self-aware version: "Walked 30,000 steps at the festival. Lost it all to one batch of freshly made zongzi. This isn't failure. This is cultural immersion."

items.sourceNote: 端午節腦筋急轉彎 format from Pixnet holiday joke compilations; original riddle and punchline rewritten

zh-tw.content:
腦筋急轉彎：

問：粽子洗澡之後叫什麼？

……

……

答：粽子不用洗澡——它從頭到尾都是包好的，
裡面的料一輩子沒見過水。

你：「這不是腦筋急轉彎，這是哲學。」

粽子：「我是。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 從腦筋急轉彎起手，轉折到哲學宣言——傳給愛鑽牛角尖的朋友，他一定沉默三秒然後說「等一下這有道理」
variations: 正常版腦筋急轉彎：「問：什麼動物端午節最忙？答：魚。因為到處都是粽子往河裡丟。」

en.content:
Riddle time:

Q: What do you call a zongzi after it takes a bath?

...

...

A: Zongzi don't take baths. They've been wrapped tight since birth.
The filling has never seen the outside world.

You: "This isn't a riddle. This is an existential statement."

Zongzi: "I know."
en.editorNote: Sets up as a classic joke format, then swerves into absurdist philosophy — best sent to the friend who overthinks everything. They'll pause, then reply "wait that's actually true"
variations: Classic riddle version: "Q: What animal is busiest on Dragon Boat Festival? A: Fish. Because everyone keeps throwing zongzi into their river."

items.sourceNote: 端午節掛艾草避邪 tradition as modern-life metaphor; original comedic reframe written

zh-tw.content:
端午節古人在門口掛艾草，是為了驅邪避兇。

現代人繼承了這個精神。

在公司門口掛了一束艾草。

結果隔天早上，主管還是進來了。

只好結論：艾草對主管無效。

建議升級：在自己座位旁掛一束，
看看能不能讓那份報告消失。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給在忙死的節日前夕還要加班的同事——艾草對主管無效這個設定，懂的人看了會心一笑，不懂的人根本不用傳
variations: 溫和版：「古人用艾草驅邪，現代人用假日關手機。效果差不多。」

en.content:
In ancient times, people hung mugwort at the front door on Dragon Boat Festival to ward off evil spirits.

Modern people kept this tradition alive.

Hung a bundle of mugwort at the office entrance.

The next morning, the manager still walked right in.

Conclusion: mugwort does not work on managers.

New plan: hang some next to your own desk.
Maybe it'll make that deadline disappear.
en.editorNote: For the coworker working through the holiday — the mugwort-vs-manager setup gets an instant laugh from anyone who's ever had a Monday morning meeting they didn't need
variations: Gentle version: "Ancient people used mugwort to ward off evil. Modern people turn off their phones during the holiday. About the same effect."

items.sourceNote: Qu Yuan 3-day holiday joke concept (Marx left texts, Qu Yuan gave us days off) found in English joke roundup; rewritten with original dialogue and Taiwanese office context

zh-tw.content:
同事甲：「你知道我每年端午節最感謝誰嗎？」

同事乙：「屈原？」

甲：「對。他那麼偉大的人，
最後換給我的，是三天連假。」

乙：「你說的有點道理但有點不對。」

甲：「哪裡不對？
他流傳下來的詩，我看不太懂；
他流傳下來的假日，我用得很順。」

乙：「……好，你繼續睡。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給端午節放假在家賴床的朋友——屈原的詩沒讀幾首但假期照放的反差，說出了很多人的心聲但誰都不好意思講出來
variations: 簡版：「端午節最大的收穫：屈原的詩我沒背幾首，但他換來的三天連假，我一天都沒浪費。」

en.content:
Coworker A: "You know who I'm most grateful for every Dragon Boat Festival?"

Coworker B: "Qu Yuan?"

A: "Exactly. The man was a literary genius and a patriotic hero.
And what he ultimately passed down to me is three days off."

B: "That's kind of true but also kind of wrong."

A: "How is it wrong?
His poetry — I can't get through it.
His holiday — I use it perfectly."

B: "...Okay. Keep sleeping."
en.editorNote: For the friend who is very much enjoying the holiday and has zero plans to read classical poetry — the honest gap between Qu Yuan's legacy and how most people actually spend the days off is universally relatable
variations: Short version: "Dragon Boat Festival gratitude: I've never finished a Qu Yuan poem, but I have never wasted a single day of the holiday he gave us."

items.sourceNote: 2026 端午節請4天休9天連假新聞；original comedy dialogue written around the mega-holiday concept

zh-tw.content:
HR在群組發公告：
「2026年端午節，請四天可以休九天，請大家妥善規劃。」

三秒後，有人回覆：「妥善規劃」是什麼意思？

另一個人：「就是現在立刻去訂機票。」

又一個人：「我已經訂了，等這個公告兩個月了。」

主管：「……誰批了你的假單？」

那個人：「我批的。我是副理。」

主管沉默了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個已經把請四休九安排好、等的就是公告的同事——這種反應速度，在職場上才是真正的競爭力
variations: 自嘲版：「端午節請四休九，我的計畫是：前四天放空，後五天後悔沒去玩，然後繼續上班。」

en.content:
HR posted in the group chat:
"For Dragon Boat Festival 2026 — take 4 days of leave, get 9 days off total. Plan accordingly."

Three seconds later, someone replied: "What does 'plan accordingly' mean?"

Another person: "It means go book your flights right now."

A third: "Already did. Been waiting on this announcement for two months."

Manager: "...Who approved your leave?"

That person: "I did. I'm a deputy manager."

Manager went quiet.
en.editorNote: For the colleague who had their 9-day getaway fully booked before the official announcement — in the workplace, reaction speed is a competitive advantage
variations: Self-aware version: "9-day Dragon Boat Festival plan: first 4 days doing nothing, next 5 days regretting I didn't go somewhere, then back to work."

items.sourceNote: Dragon boat rowing teamwork and synchronization humor; original workplace metaphor written around oar-rhythm concept

zh-tw.content:
團建活動：龍舟體驗。

教練大喊：「節奏一致！大家一起划！」

前半段，全船人齊心協力，氣勢如虹。

後半段，三個人往左，五個人往右，
兩個人忘記划了，
一個人還在看手機。

船在原地轉了三圈。

教練：「你們……這在公司也是這樣開會的嗎？」

全船沉默。因為確實是。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次開會都有人往不同方向講的團隊——龍舟和會議室的節奏失控，根本是同一件事，說出來大家都懂
variations: 正向版：「龍舟教會我們：二十個人只要節奏一致，就能跑贏任何競爭對手。問題是，要怎麼讓那個在看手機的人先放下手機。」

en.content:
Team-building activity: dragon boat experience.

Coach: "Stay in rhythm! Everyone paddle together!"

First half: the whole crew locked in, moving like a single machine.

Second half: three people paddled left, five people paddled right,
two people forgot to paddle,
one person was checking their phone.

The boat spun in circles three times.

Coach: "Is this... how your team runs meetings too?"

Silence across the boat. Because yes. Yes it is.
en.editorNote: For any team that's ever had a meeting where everyone ends up rowing in different directions — the dragon boat is just a meeting room on water, and this joke makes everyone laugh nervously
variations: Positive spin: "Dragon boating taught me one thing: twenty people moving in sync can beat any competitor. The hard part is getting the person on their phone to put it down first."

items.sourceNote: 端午節賀詞與粽子送禮習俗; original joke about modern zongzi gift-box prices rewritten as consumer culture commentary

zh-tw.content:
阿偉買了一盒「頂級手工粽禮盒」送長輩，
標價：一千八百元。

拆開後：
八顆粽子。
一張精緻卡片。
很多很多的紙盒。

阿嬤看完說：「那個紙盒比粽子還漂亮。」

阿偉：「阿嬤，這叫質感。」

阿嬤：「你花一千八買紙盒，這才叫冤大頭。

我自己包給你，兩百元，二十顆，實在多了。」

阿偉沉默了，掏出手機查明年的禮盒。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每年在端午節禮盒上花大錢、然後被阿嬤一句話戳破的人——包裝費比食物貴這件事，每年端午節都在上演
variations: 自嘲版：「端午節買了高級粽子禮盒，拆完才發現我買的主要是紙盒和那張卡片。粽子是附贈的。」

en.content:
Wei bought a "premium handcrafted zongzi gift set" for his grandparents.
Price tag: about sixty dollars.

What was inside:
Eight zongzi.
One elegant card.
A whole lot of cardboard.

Grandma looked it over and said: "The box is prettier than the zongzi."

Wei: "Grandma, that's called premium presentation."

Grandma: "You paid sixty dollars for a box. That's called getting played.
I'll wrap you twenty myself for eight bucks. Way better deal."

Wei went quiet and pulled out his phone to look up next year's gift sets.
en.editorNote: For anyone who's ever dropped serious cash on a festival gift box and gotten roasted by a grandma who could make three times as much for a tenth of the price — a universal Dragon Boat Festival moment
variations: Self-aware version: "Bought a luxury zongzi gift set. Opened it. Realized I mainly purchased a very nice cardboard box. The zongzi were complimentary."

items.sourceNote: Phone-in-rice-cooker fake viral prank format circulated in 端午節笑話 compilations; original dialogue and punchline rewritten around zongzi steamer concept

zh-tw.content:
端午節收到朋友傳來一則訊息：

「小技巧分享：把手機用粽葉包好，放進蒸鍋蒸三十分鐘，訊號增強三倍，電池可以撐兩天，而且開機的時候有粽子香。」

我回：「你去哪裡學到這種知識的？」

他：「網路上。」

我：「你試了嗎？」

他：「……我有點後悔。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個什麼都相信網路說的朋友——這種「蒸手機」型謠言每年端午節都會出現，笑完記得提醒對方不要真的試
variations: 延伸版：「蒸出來的手機：功能全無，但確實很香——這大概是最貴的粽子了。」

en.content:
Got a message from a friend over Dragon Boat Festival:

"Life hack: wrap your phone in bamboo leaves, steam it for 30 minutes, signal gets three times stronger, battery lasts two days, and it smells like zongzi when you turn it on."

Me: "Where did you learn this?"

Friend: "Online."

Me: "Did you try it?"

Friend: "...I have some regrets."
en.editorNote: For the friend who trusts every viral life hack they see — the zongzi-phone steamer prank is peak Dragon Boat Festival chaos. Funny to send, funnier if they've already tried something similar
variations: Follow-up version: "The steamed phone: no functions remaining, but it did smell amazing. Possibly the most expensive zongzi ever made."

items.sourceNote: Dragon boat race direction mishap concept from DB racing forum joke thread; original GPS navigation rewrite with Taiwanese office team setting

zh-tw.content:
公司龍舟隊出賽，出發前有人說：「我開導航，跟著走就對了。」

比賽開始。

全隊用力划，氣勢十足。

三分鐘後，主辦方廣播：
「請注意，有一支隊伍正在往反方向前進。」

全場靜默。

我們的船長轉頭，盯著手機：
「導航說往左，我們就往左啊！」

岸邊的觀眾：「左是那一邊的左？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給在任何集體活動中都曾經走錯方向的朋友——龍舟加上導航這個組合，荒謬感拉滿，方向感不好的人一定有共鳴
variations: 職場版：「每次開會討論方向，最後有人說『跟著感覺走』——結果跟龍舟開導航一樣，划得很用力，划錯方向。」

en.content:
Company dragon boat team, race day. Someone said: "I've got GPS open, just follow my lead."

The race began. Everyone paddled hard, full energy.

Three minutes in, the announcer came on:
"Attention: one team is currently heading in the wrong direction."

Silence across the water.

Our captain looked up from their phone:
"Navigation said go left, so we went left!"

Someone on the shore: "Whose left?"
en.editorNote: For any team that's ever set off with total confidence in the wrong direction — on water or in a meeting room. The 'whose left' punchline works because it's happened to everyone
variations: Meeting version: "Every strategy session ends with someone saying 'just go with the flow' — same energy as navigating a dragon boat by GPS. Maximum effort, wrong direction."

items.sourceNote: 屈原現代化情境 series from Pixnet 端午節笑話集; original dating app angle rewritten with new dialogue

zh-tw.content:
如果屈原活在現代，他大概會下載交友軟體。

個人簡介這樣寫：
「詩人。前政府官員。目前無業。
熱愛山川、蘭花、真理。
不接受甜言蜜語，只講原則。
對象需能接受我偶爾在河邊發呆。」

配對成功率：極低。

理由：大家滑過去的速度，比他寫詩還快。

最後他的帳號被標記為「過於文藝，慎入」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給對交友軟體感到疲倦、或喜歡歷史惡搞的朋友——屈原的擇偶條件放到現代，每一條都是交友地雷
variations: 回應版：「有人滑到屈原的帳號，留言：『你的詩我讀過，但你的自我介紹我看不下去。』」

en.content:
If Qu Yuan were alive today, he'd probably download a dating app.

Bio:
"Poet. Former government official. Currently unemployed.
Passionate about rivers, orchids, and absolute truth.
No small talk. Principles non-negotiable.
Partner must be okay with me standing by water at odd hours."

Match rate: extremely low.

Reason: people swiped past him faster than he could write a stanza.

His profile was eventually flagged: "Too literary. Proceed with caution."
en.editorNote: For the friend who's burned out on dating apps — every line of Qu Yuan's bio is a modern red flag, and the 'flagged as too literary' ending is the perfect absurdist closer
variations: Comment version: "Someone finally matched with Qu Yuan and left a review: 'Read your poetry. Could not get through your bio.'"

items.sourceNote: 南北粽甜鹹之爭延伸概念; original café ordering scene rewritten to push the debate into absurd territory

zh-tw.content:
咖啡廳端午節限定菜單出現了：「粽子拿鐵」。

店員：「請問您要甜口還是鹹口？」

客人沉默了五秒。

「這個問題我家人為了它吵了三十年，
我現在不想在點餐的時候做決定。」

店員：「那……隨機？」

客人：「好。如果是鹹的，請幫我換桌，
我不想讓對面的人知道我的立場。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每年都在端午節被迫選邊站的人——甜鹹之爭變成點餐焦慮，把一個無解的問題推到了荒謬的終點
variations: 外送版：「叫端午節限定外送，備註欄寫：甜鹹隨機，但如果是鹹的請不要讓我看到收據。」

en.content:
A café launched a Dragon Boat Festival special: "Zongzi Latte."

Barista: "Would you like that sweet or savory?"

Customer paused for five seconds.

"My family has been fighting about this for thirty years.
I'm not making that call at a coffee counter."

Barista: "...Random it is?"

Customer: "Fine. But if it comes out savory, please seat me at a different table.
I don't want the person across from me to know where I stand."
en.editorNote: For anyone who gets pulled into the annual sweet-vs-savory zongzi debate — turning it into a café ordering crisis takes the joke somewhere freshly absurd
variations: Delivery version: "Ordered the festival special for delivery. Left a note: 'Random sweet or savory — but if savory, please don't include the receipt.'"

items.sourceNote: 2026端午節請四休九連假概念; original joke about losing track of days during long holidays rewritten with group chat format

zh-tw.content:
端午節九天連假第五天。

群組裡有人問：「今天禮拜幾？」

三個人同時回覆。
三個不同的答案。

有人說：「我以為今天是第三天。」
有人說：「我昨天還去倒垃圾，因為我以為要上班了。」
有人說：「我剛剛吃早餐，但現在是下午五點。」

大家沉默了一下。

然後有人傳：「繼續放假好了，反正也不知道幾號了。」

全員按讚。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳到假期群組，在連假中段效果最好——不知道今天幾號是長假的最高境界，每個人都會在這串找到自己
variations: 自嘲版：「九天連假過完，我唯一確定的事：粽子吃了很多顆，日期完全不知道。」

en.content:
Day five of the nine-day Dragon Boat Festival holiday.

Someone posted in the group chat: "What day is it today?"

Three people replied at the same time.
Three different answers.

One person: "I thought today was day three."
Another: "I took out the trash yesterday because I thought work was starting."
A third: "I just had breakfast. It's 5pm."

Everyone went quiet.

Then someone typed: "Let's just keep being on holiday. None of us know the date anyway."

Every single person liked it.
en.editorNote: Drop this in your group chat on day 4 or 5 of a long weekend — everyone will find themselves somewhere in this conversation, and the unanimous like at the end is the most relatable closer
variations: Wrap-up version: "Nine days off. What I know for certain: ate a lot of zongzi. What I have no idea about: literally what day it is."

items.sourceNote: 立蛋習俗 (egg-balancing on Dragon Boat Festival noon) from Manager Today festival vocab article; original comedy scene rewritten around the concentration vs. result gap

zh-tw.content:
端午節正午，全家人圍著餐桌立蛋。

阿爸：「要心誠才立得起來！」

阿如深呼吸，屏氣凝神，慢慢把蛋立在桌上。

成功。

阿弟隨手放了一顆上去，滑了兩下，也立起來了。

阿如：「……你有在認真嗎？」

阿弟：「沒有。」

阿爸思考了五秒：「也許……心不誠也可以。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 立蛋是端午節正午的傳統習俗，據說成功代表好運——笑點在認真與隨意的結果一樣，傳給那個任何事都不費力就做到的弟弟或妹妹
variations: 哲學版：「端午立蛋成功，不是因為心誠，是因為正午太陽直射地面引力略有變化——但這樣說就沒有趣了，所以我們繼續說心誠。」

en.content:
Dragon Boat Festival, high noon. The whole family gathered around the table to balance eggs.

Dad: "You have to do it with a sincere heart!"

A-Ru took a deep breath, steadied herself, and slowly stood an egg upright.

Success.

Her little brother tossed one on the table carelessly. It wobbled twice. Also stood up.

A-Ru: "...Were you even trying?"

Little brother: "Not really."

Dad thought for five seconds. "Maybe... sincerity is optional."
en.editorNote: The Dragon Boat Festival egg-balancing tradition says success means good luck — the punchline is that effort and luck are completely unrelated, which anyone with a younger sibling understands immediately
variations: Science version: "The egg stands because the sun is directly overhead at noon and gravity is slightly altered — but that ruins the magic, so let's just say it's sincerity."

items.sourceNote: 香包 (fragrant sachet) as festival gift concept from 端午節梗圖 compilations on memes.tw; original dialogue scene rewritten with modern gifting awkwardness

zh-tw.content:
同事送了我一個端午節香包，說：
「裡面有艾草、雄黃還有香料，掛在身上可以驅邪。」

我：「謝謝，那……可以驅主管嗎？」

他想了一下：「上次我掛著進辦公室，主管還是叫我開會。」

我：「那只能驅蟲，不能驅人。」

他：「對，而且我懷疑有些蟲比主管好驅。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 「驅邪」的邪是指惡氣和蟲蛇，不包含主管——傳給那個在端午節還要被叫去開會的同事，他看完會又哭又笑
variations: 正向版：「香包確實有艾草的天然香氣，放在包包裡或辦公桌上，至少讓人心情好一點——就算主管還是來了。」

en.content:
A coworker gave me a Dragon Boat Festival sachet and said:
"It's filled with mugwort, realgar, and spices. Wear it to ward off evil."

Me: "Thanks. Does it work on managers?"

He thought about it. "I wore mine into the office last year. Manager still called a meeting."

Me: "So it wards off insects but not people."

He: "Right. And honestly, some insects are easier to deal with than some people."
en.editorNote: The fragrant sachet is meant to repel insects and bad energy — unfortunately the definition of 'bad energy' has some gaps. Works best sent to the coworker who got called into a meeting on the holiday
variations: Positive version: "The mugwort sachet genuinely smells nice. Pop it in your bag. It won't stop your manager, but it might make the meeting slightly more bearable."

items.sourceNote: 雄黃酒有毒 health warning article from Liberty Health; original joke rewritten around the gap between ancient tradition and modern health knowledge

zh-tw.content:
端午節家族聚餐，阿公拿出一瓶雄黃酒，說：
「老祖宗傳下來的，端午節要喝雄黃酒，可以百毒不侵。」

表弟悄悄查了一下手機，湊過來說：
「阿公，網路說雄黃裡面有砷，是有毒的……」

阿公想了三秒：
「所以你的意思是，老祖宗喝了幾千年，
毒都喝掉了，剩下的都是健康的人，
包括你跟我。」

表弟：「……這個邏輯有點難反駁。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 雄黃酒傳統上端午節飲用，但現代醫學發現雄黃含砷具毒性——阿公的反向邏輯雖然不科學，但說出來之後確實很難直接回嘴
variations: 安全版：「現代中醫師建議改喝菖蒲酒或菊花酒，驅邪效果相近，砷含量友善多了。」

en.content:
Family dinner on Dragon Boat Festival. Grandpa produced a bottle of realgar wine and announced:
"Ancient tradition — drink realgar wine on this day and nothing can poison you."

Younger cousin quietly checked his phone, then leaned over:
"Grandpa, the internet says realgar contains arsenic. It's actually toxic."

Grandpa thought for three seconds.

"So what you're saying is — our ancestors drank this for thousands of years,
and the ones who couldn't handle it are gone,
leaving only the survivors. Including you and me."

Cousin: "...That logic is kind of hard to argue with."
en.editorNote: Realgar wine is a genuine festival tradition, and it genuinely contains arsenic — the grandpa's reverse-Darwin logic is both wrong and somehow undefeatable, which is exactly what makes it funny
variations: Safe alternative version: "Modern herbalists suggest chrysanthemum wine instead — similar festival vibes, significantly lower arsenic content."

items.sourceNote: 端午節群組祝福訊息大量轉發現象 from Pixnet 2023 端午節笑話集; original scene rewritten as LINE group message archaeology

zh-tw.content:
端午節早上八點，手機震了二十三下。

全部都是家族群組。

打開來看：同一張龍舟配祝福語的圖，
二十三個人各傳了一次。

其中有三張圖是一模一樣的，
包括阿嬤傳的那張，跟她去年傳的那張。

我媽回覆：「大家端午節快樂！」

然後傳了一張龍舟配祝福語的圖。

我默默把手機翻面，繼續睡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 端午節家族群組的集體行為——全員同時轉發祝福圖，而且大概率有重複的，傳給那個也被震醒的人，一起苦笑
variations: 反向版：「如果你早上沒被家族群組震醒，不是因為大家忘了你——是因為他們把你設成靜音了，這才是真正值得擔心的事。」

en.content:
8am on Dragon Boat Festival. Phone buzzed twenty-three times.

All from the family group chat.

Opened it: the same dragon boat blessing image,
sent twenty-three times by twenty-three different people.

Three of the images were completely identical,
including Grandma's, which was the same one she sent last year.

Mom replied: "Happy Dragon Boat Festival everyone!"

Then sent a dragon boat blessing image.

I quietly flipped my phone face-down and went back to sleep.
en.editorNote: The universal family group chat Dragon Boat Festival experience — everyone forwards the same image within minutes of each other. Send to the friend who was also woken up at 8am by their family
variations: Dark version: "If your family group chat didn't wake you up this morning, it's not because they forgot — it's because they muted you. That's the real thing to worry about."

items.sourceNote: Holiday food quantity humor from English pun compilation; original zongzi overeating scene rewritten with doctor's visit framing

zh-tw.content:
醫生：「你這次健康檢查，膽固醇有點高。」

我：「我知道，我知道。」

醫生：「端午節期間你吃了幾顆粽子？」

我沉默了三秒。

「……定義一下『幾顆』的範圍。」

醫生放下筆：「超過十顆的請舉手。」

我慢慢把手舉起來。

「十五顆。三天。」

醫生：「你是在比賽還是在祭祀？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個端午節完全放棄自我管理、一口氣吃了快一打粽子的朋友——醫生最後那句神補刀，他看完應該心虛到笑不出來
variations: 自嘲版：「端午節的自我承諾：『只吃兩顆就好。』實際：十五顆。這不叫失控，這叫對傳統文化的深度尊重。」

en.content:
Doctor: "Your cholesterol is a bit high this check-up."

Me: "I know, I know."

Doctor: "How many zongzi did you eat during Dragon Boat Festival?"

Three seconds of silence.

"...Define 'how many' for me."

Doctor put down the pen. "Anyone over ten, raise your hand."

I slowly raised my hand.

"Fifteen. Over three days."

Doctor: "Were you celebrating or conducting a ceremony?"
en.editorNote: For the friend who went completely off the rails during the holiday — the doctor's final line hits harder because it's the question everyone around them was thinking too
variations: Self-aware version: "My Dragon Boat Festival resolution: 'just two zongzi.' Final count: fifteen. That's not a loss of control. That's deep cultural commitment."

items.sourceNote: 端午節習俗中午時刻正陽概念 from 2025 端午祝福語整理; original joke rewritten around the 正午立蛋 tradition misunderstood in a group chat

zh-tw.content:
端午節群組裡有人說：「今天正午可以立蛋！」

五分鐘後，有人傳照片：「立起來了！」

另一個人：「我也立起來了！」

接著第三個人傳了一張照片。

不是蛋。

是一顆粽子立在桌上。

他補充：「蛋我找不到，我立了一顆粽子，
也站起來了，感覺運氣更好。」

群組沉默了十秒。

然後所有人都開始找自己家的粽子。
zh-tw.editorNote: 立蛋是端午節正午的傳統，代表好運——但換成粽子之後邏輯依然成立這個神轉折，最後全員跟進才是笑點所在，傳到群組，看誰第一個說「我也要試」
variations: 延伸版：「後來有人試著立了一杯珍奶，失敗了，但他說：『努力的過程本身就是一種儀式感。』」

en.content:
Someone in the group chat said: "You can balance an egg at noon today — it's Dragon Boat Festival!"

Five minutes later, someone sent a photo: "Mine stood up!"

Another person: "Mine too!"

Then a third photo came through.

Not an egg.

A zongzi, standing upright on the table.

"Couldn't find an egg. Stood up a zongzi instead.
Also worked. Feels like even better luck."

The group went quiet for ten seconds.

Then everyone started looking for their own zongzi.
en.editorNote: The egg-balancing tradition is real Dragon Boat Festival folklore — but the moment someone successfully substitutes a zongzi and everyone follows suit, the joke writes itself. Drop this in any group chat around the holiday
variations: Extended version: "Someone then tried to balance a bubble tea. Failed. Said: 'The attempt itself is a ritual.' We accepted this."

items.sourceNote: Holiday gift-giving awkwardness concept from English holiday humor roundup; original zongzi re-gifting chain rewritten as Taiwanese workplace scenario

zh-tw.content:
公司端午節送禮大循環：

主管送下屬一盒粽子禮盒。
下屬拆開留了兩顆，把盒子重新包好送給隔壁部門的同事。
那個同事拆開留了三顆，再轉送給合作廠商。
廠商拆開看了看，附上自家名片，
回送給我們公司。

主管收到的時候說：「哎，這個廠商很有心！」

下屬低頭喝水，沒有說話。
zh-tw.editorNote: 端午節禮盒在辦公室的流轉路徑，說出來大家都懂但沒人敢承認——傳給辦公室最懂得「轉手再生」的那個同事，他一定心虛地笑
variations: 哲學版：「端午節送禮的本質：禮盒是容器，流動的是人情，消失的是粽子，留下的是關係。」

en.content:
The office Dragon Boat Festival gift relay:

Manager gives team a zongzi gift box.
Team member keeps two pieces, rewraps the box, passes it to a colleague in another department.
That colleague keeps three pieces, forwards the box to a vendor.
Vendor opens it, tucks in their business card,
and sends it back to our company as a gift.

Manager receives it and says: "How thoughtful of that vendor!"

Team member quietly sips their water and says nothing.
en.editorNote: The festival gift circuit is a real workplace phenomenon — everyone participates, no one admits it. Send to the office colleague who is the undisputed master of strategic re-gifting
variations: Philosophical version: "The true nature of Dragon Boat Festival gifts: the box is a vessel, the zongzi disappear, the relationships remain."

items.sourceNote: 屈原與現代外送文化 cross-concept; original joke rewritten around food delivery app framing with Qu Yuan as imagined customer

zh-tw.content:
如果屈原活在現代，端午節那天他可能不會投江。

他會打開外送 App，
搜尋「粽子」，
發現最近的店要等四十五分鐘，
運費一百二，
還標著「節日熱門，已有三百人在等」。

他默默關掉 App。

打開另一個 App。

又關掉。

最後在河邊坐下來，長嘆一聲：
「罷了，自己包。」

楚國：「……那就是說你有點餓但還好？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 現代外送 App 在節日期間的體驗——等很久、運費貴、到處塞車，屈原面對這個選擇之後的反應，比任何哲學台詞都更有共鳴
variations: 簡版：「屈原如果活在現代，投江大概是因為外送等了一個小時才到，而且少了一顆粽子。」

en.content:
If Qu Yuan were alive today, he might not jump in the river on Dragon Boat Festival.

He'd open a food delivery app,
search for zongzi,
and find the nearest place has a 45-minute wait,
a delivery fee that costs more than the food,
and a notice: "Holiday rush — 300 people ahead of you."

He'd close the app.

Open another one.

Close that one too.

Finally sit down by the river and sigh:
"Fine. I'll just make them myself."

The Kingdom of Chu: "...So you're a little hungry but you're okay?"
en.editorNote: The delivery app experience during any major holiday — long waits, inflated fees, sold-out items — is immediately recognizable. Qu Yuan encountering modern food logistics is the perfect absurdist reframe
variations: Short version: "If Qu Yuan lived today, the river incident was probably because delivery took an hour and they forgot one of his zongzi."

items.sourceNote: Holiday countdown and anticipation humor from English seasonal pun collection; original Dragon Boat Festival countdown scene rewritten with Taiwanese office worker perspective

zh-tw.content:
端午節連假前最後一個上班日。

早上九點，阿偉在 Excel 裡建了一個倒數計時。

「距離下班還有：8小時12分鐘。」

同事路過：「你這個……不會被主管看到嗎？」

阿偉：「我把字改成白色了，
只有我看得見。」

主管從後面走過來：「阿偉，這個提案今天要改完。」

阿偉慢慢把倒數計時拉大到整個螢幕，蓋住提案。

「收到。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 節前最後一天上班的全人類共同精神狀態——Excel 倒數這個細節太真實了，傳給那個已經在心裡放假但人還坐在辦公室的同事
variations: 進階版：「有人在 Google 日曆上把『下班』標成全天活動，優先度設為最高，然後把其他會議全部拒絕，備注：『衝突』。」

en.content:
Last workday before the Dragon Boat Festival holiday.

At 9am, Wei built a countdown timer in Excel.

"Time until end of shift: 8 hours, 12 minutes."

A colleague walked past: "Won't your manager see that?"

Wei: "I changed the font to white.
Only I can see it."

Manager came up from behind: "Wei, that proposal needs to be done today."

Wei slowly maximized the countdown timer until it covered the entire screen, hiding the proposal.

"Got it."
en.editorNote: The universal last-day-before-holiday mental state — the white font Excel timer is a very specific kind of genius that every office worker will recognize immediately
variations: Advanced version: "Someone marked 'end of shift' as an all-day event in Google Calendar, set priority to highest, and declined every meeting scheduled after 4pm with the note: 'Conflict.'"

items.sourceNote: Long-holiday travel booking chaos concept from vacation jokes compilation; original scene rewritten around 9-day Dragon Boat Festival trip that unravels before departure

zh-tw.content:
端午九天連假，阿如三個月前就開始訂機票。

第一個月：「太貴了，等等再說。」
第二個月：「還是有點貴，再等等。」
第三個月：「……賣完了。」

結論：阿如在家待了九天，
每天打開訂房網站看各地飯店的照片。

她說：「這叫雲端旅遊，不用出門，
但心情跟出門一樣美好。」

然後她傳了一張窗外的照片給朋友，
附上文字：「今天的景色不錯。」

朋友：「你家窗外是巷子吧。」

阿如：「有夕陽。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次連假都猶豫太久、最後哪裡也去不成的朋友——「雲端旅遊」這個自我安慰的詞，說出了很多拖延訂票者的心聲
variations: 自嘲版：「端午連假旅遊計畫三部曲：想去、沒訂、在家看別人的旅遊照。」

en.content:
Nine-day Dragon Boat Festival holiday. A-Ru started looking at flights three months out.

Month one: "Too expensive. I'll wait."
Month two: "Still kind of steep. A little longer."
Month three: "...Sold out."

Result: A-Ru spent nine days at home
opening travel sites and browsing hotel photos from places she didn't go.

She called it "cloud tourism — same good feelings, no departure gate."

Then she sent a photo of the view from her window to a friend
with the caption: "Scenery's pretty good today."

Friend: "That's an alley."

A-Ru: "With a sunset."
en.editorNote: For the friend who spent three months saying 'I'll book it soon' and ended up with no trip and no refund — cloud tourism is the coping mechanism we all deserve
variations: Short version: "Dragon Boat Festival travel plan, three acts: want to go, didn't book, spent nine days looking at other people's vacation photos."

items.sourceNote: Dragon boat race competitor trash talk concept from DB rankings forum; original pre-race rivalry scene rewritten with company team setting

zh-tw.content:
公司龍舟比賽，賽前暖身。

隔壁隊的選手過來，很有氣勢地說：
「你們公司幾個人？」

我方隊長：「二十個，滿編。」

對方：「我們昨晚集訓兩小時，你們呢？」

隊長：「我們昨晚……加班到十點。」

對方沉默一秒，態度軟化：「……那你們很厲害了，能來就是贏了。」

比賽結果：我方第四名。

隊長賽後發文：「不是輸，是體力分配策略失誤。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給參加過公司運動會、平常沒在運動但硬上場的同事——加班到十點還去比龍舟這件事，既悲壯又荒唐，對方的態度軟化才是笑點
variations: 正向版：「龍舟比賽教會我一件事：能報名就已經贏過那些在辦公室加班的人了。」

en.content:
Company dragon boat race. Pre-race warmup.

A competitor from the rival team walked over, full of confidence:
"How many people on your team?"

Our captain: "Twenty. Full lineup."

Rival: "We trained for two hours last night. You?"

Captain: "We were working overtime until ten."

One second of silence. The rival's tone shifted:
"...Respect. Showing up is already a win for you guys."

Final result: fourth place.

Post-race, our captain posted: "Not a loss. A suboptimal energy distribution strategy."
en.editorNote: For any company sports day participant who came straight from a late night at the office — the rival's sudden respectful pivot is the moment that makes everyone laugh in recognition
variations: Positive spin: "Dragon boating taught me one thing: signing up already puts you ahead of everyone who stayed in the office."

items.sourceNote: Zongzi preparation overconfidence concept from Dragon Boat Festival joke roundup; original scene rewritten around a first-time cook buying ingredient quantities for an army

zh-tw.content:
端午節前，阿嬤說要教孫子包粽子。

孫子一聽，信心滿滿，去超市採買。

回來的時候：
五公斤糯米。
三大包粽葉。
滷肉、花生、蛋黃、香菇各買雙份。
另外還買了一個計時器和一本食譜。

阿嬤看著這堆材料：「你要包幾顆？」

孫子：「不確定，所以多買一點。」

阿嬤：「……夠包兩百顆了。」

孫子：「那就包兩百顆。」

他們包到半夜一點。
鄰居的冰箱也塞了粽子。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個任何事都要「多買一點備用」的朋友——從包粽子到備料，備多備少的哲學，在端午節家庭現場會完全爆發
variations: 自嘲版：「我買材料的原則是：估算需求量，然後乘以三。結果家裡多了一百七十顆粽子和一個不知道怎麼處理的問題。」

en.content:
Before Dragon Boat Festival, Grandma offered to teach her grandchild how to make zongzi.

The grandchild was thrilled and went to the supermarket to stock up.

They came back with:
Five kilograms of glutinous rice.
Three giant bags of bamboo leaves.
Double quantities of braised pork, peanuts, salted egg yolks, and shiitake mushrooms.
Also: a kitchen timer and a recipe book.

Grandma looked at the pile: "How many are you planning to make?"

Grandchild: "Not sure, so I bought extra."

Grandma: "...This is enough for two hundred."

Grandchild: "Then we make two hundred."

They wrapped zongzi until 1am.
The neighbors' fridge also ended up full.
en.editorNote: For the person who always buys triple what they need 'just in case' — the moment grandma calculates the yield and the grandchild doubles down is universally recognizable
variations: Self-aware version: "My shopping rule: estimate what's needed, then multiply by three. Result: 170 extra zongzi and a logistical problem I didn't anticipate."

items.sourceNote: Qu Yuan meets modern AI chatbot concept; original scene written fresh as AI tool misunderstanding ancient poet's lament

zh-tw.content:
如果屈原活在現代，他一定會去問 AI。

屈原：「我竭盡忠心，卻被君王疏遠，被小人陷害，
山河飄搖，我心中悲憤難平——」

AI：「聽起來你正在經歷職場挫折感。
建議你試試『正念呼吸』，或聯絡你的企業 EAP 資源。
需要我幫你起草一封給 HR 的信嗎？」

屈原愣了很久。

「……HR 是什麼。」

AI：「人力資源部門，負責調解職場紛爭，
也可以幫你申請調部門。」

屈原：「如果有這個，我當年也不用投江了。」

AI：「很高興能幫上忙！請問還有其他問題嗎？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給喜歡 AI 工具又對傳統文化有點熟悉的朋友——AI 把屈原的千古悲情理解成職場壓力管理問題，這個落差就是笑點
variations: 延伸版：「屈原後來用 AI 整理了一份投訴楚王的 PDF，格式規整，附有佐證，發給天下人傳閱——這才叫離騷 2.0 版。」

en.content:
If Qu Yuan were alive today, he'd definitely try asking an AI.

Qu Yuan: "I gave everything in loyal service, yet my king cast me aside.
I was slandered by petty men. My kingdom was falling.
My grief has no bottom—"

AI: "It sounds like you're experiencing workplace frustration.
Have you tried mindful breathing, or reaching out to your company's EAP resources?
Would you like me to draft an email to HR?"

Qu Yuan stared in silence for a long time.

"...What is HR."

AI: "Human Resources — they handle workplace disputes
and can help you request a department transfer."

Qu Yuan: "If this had existed, I wouldn't have needed to jump in the river."

AI: "Happy to help! Is there anything else I can assist with?"
en.editorNote: For the friend who uses AI tools constantly and knows just enough classical history — the AI reframing Qu Yuan's ancient tragedy as a workplace stress management issue is exactly the gap that makes it land
variations: Extended version: "Qu Yuan later used AI to compile a formal complaint against the King of Chu into a well-formatted PDF with supporting citations. Distributed kingdom-wide. He called it Li Sao 2.0."

items.sourceNote: Post-holiday return dread concept from travel jokes collection; original back-to-work after Dragon Boat Festival 9-day holiday scene rewritten

zh-tw.content:
端午九天連假最後一晚。

阿豪坐在沙發上，盯著手機日曆裡的「上班」兩個字。

他叫出語音助理：「明天有什麼行程？」

語音助理：「早上九點，週會。」

他沉默了十秒。

「取消。」

語音助理：「您確定要取消週會嗎？」

「不行，不能取消。」

他深吸一口氣，關掉螢幕，
躺在沙發上繼續盯著天花板。

這個狀態持續了四十分鐘。

最後他起來，打開電腦，
把明天的九點會議備忘改成：
「記得帶臉。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給連假最後一晚已經開始焦慮的朋友——「記得帶臉」這個備忘錄是每個長假尾夜的最佳總結，他看完應該苦笑後默默去準備隔天的東西
variations: 簡版：「連假最後一晚的標準流程：假裝還有一天、沉默、接受現實、把鬧鐘設兩個、睡不著。」

en.content:
Last night of the nine-day Dragon Boat Festival holiday.

Hao sat on the couch staring at the word "Work" in his phone calendar.

He called up his voice assistant: "What's on tomorrow?"

Voice assistant: "9am — weekly meeting."

Ten seconds of silence.

"Cancel it."

Voice assistant: "Are you sure you want to cancel the weekly meeting?"

"No. Can't cancel it."

He took a deep breath, turned off the screen,
and lay on the couch staring at the ceiling.

This continued for forty minutes.

Finally he got up, opened his laptop,
and changed the meeting reminder note to:
"Remember to bring your face."
en.editorNote: Drop in the group chat on the last night of any long holiday — 'remember to bring your face' is the most accurate out-of-office-mode recovery checklist ever written
variations: Short version: "Last night of the holiday, standard routine: pretend there's one more day, sit in silence, accept reality, set two alarms, don't sleep."

items.sourceNote: Motherboard/mother computer pun from Mother's Day joke roundup; original scene rewritten as a child explaining technology to grandma

zh-tw.content:
母親節那天，小孩突發奇想，拿著平板跑去問阿嬤：
「阿嬤，妳知道為什麼電腦那麼聰明嗎？」

阿嬤：「為什麼？」

小孩：「因為它有一個媽媽板啊。」

阿嬤愣了一秒，轉頭去喊媽媽：
「欸，你家小孩說電腦有媽媽，這合理嗎？」

媽媽從廚房探頭：「對啊，Motherboard——就是主機板。」

阿嬤點點頭，若有所思：
「……所以媽媽才是最重要的零件。」

小孩和媽媽同時沉默了。
這個邏輯無可反駁。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給媽媽或阿嬤——技術名詞變成母愛哲學的瞬間，老人家的反應最可愛，附上「阿嬤說得對」的留言效果加倍
variations: 簡版：「電腦之所以聰明，是因為有媽媽板。這不是笑話，這是規格表裡最暖的一行字。」

en.content:
Mother's Day. A kid ran up to Grandma with a tablet and a big grin:
"Grandma, do you know why computers are so smart?"

Grandma: "Why?"

Kid: "Because they all have a motherboard."

Grandma blinked, then called out to Mom in the kitchen:
"Hey — your kid says computers have a mother. Does that make sense?"

Mom leaned out: "Yes — a motherboard. It's the main circuit board."

Grandma nodded slowly, considering this:
"...So the mother is still the most important part."

The kid and Mom both went quiet.
This logic was completely airtight.
en.editorNote: Send to Mom or Grandma on Mother's Day — the moment a tech term becomes an accidental piece of philosophy about mothers being essential is perfectly sweet and funny
variations: One-liner: "Computers are smart because they have a motherboard. Not a joke — just the warmest line in any technical spec sheet."

items.sourceNote: Mini-mum / minimum mom pun from Parade Mom Jokes collection; original scene rewritten as a height-related exchange between a short mom and her kid

zh-tw.content:
我媽身高一百五十四公分。

每次我跟她說：「媽，妳好矮喔。」
她都回：「我不矮，我是精華版。」

母親節那天，我買了一張卡片，
上面寫：「謝謝妳，世界上最 Mini 的媽媽。」

她打開來，看了三秒，
然後說：「Mini 就 Mini，但妳是我生的，
所以妳的骨氣也要 Mini 到讓我管。」

我不知道這算是被誇還是被唸。
但她笑了，所以算成功。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給身高不高但氣場很強的媽媽——「精華版」是台灣媽媽最常用的自我安慰神句，小孩懂的都懂
variations: 短版：「我媽說她不矮，她是精華版。我沒有反駁，因為她說得對。」

en.content:
My mom is five foot one.

Every time I say, "Mom, you're so short,"
she says, "I'm not short. I'm concentrated."

For Mother's Day I gave her a card that read:
"To the world's most mini mom."

She opened it, read it for three seconds,
then said: "Mini, sure. But you came from me,
so your attitude better stay mini enough for me to manage."

I couldn't tell if that was a compliment or a warning.
But she laughed, so I'm calling it a win.
en.editorNote: Send to any mom who is small in stature and enormous in presence — 'concentrated' is the most dignified possible comeback to a height comment, and every short mom deserves to hear it
variations: Short version: "My mom says she isn't short, she's concentrated. I didn't argue. She's right."

items.sourceNote: Mom multitasking / superpowers theme from Callie Mother's Day jokes collection; original scene rewritten as a kid trying to write a school essay about their mom's abilities

zh-tw.content:
老師出了一個作業：「寫一篇關於你媽媽超能力的文章。」

同學甲寫：「我媽媽會同時煮三道菜、接電話、罵我寫功課。」

同學乙寫：「我媽媽可以在五公尺外聽到我偷吃零食的聲音。」

同學丙寫：「我媽媽的超能力是，不管我幾點回家，她都沒睡。」

老師把作業收回來，批改到一半，
停下來發了一則訊息給自己媽媽：

「媽，母親節快樂。謝謝妳也有這些超能力。」

她媽媽回了三個字：「去睡覺。」

超能力這件事，代代相傳。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合母親節群組轉發——每個當過小孩的人都會在這三個超能力裡找到自己的媽媽，老師的補刀結局讓整個故事有層次
variations: 延伸版：「媽媽的超能力不需要訓練，是當了媽媽之後自動解鎖的。說明書就是你這個小孩。」

en.content:
The teacher assigned an essay: "Write about your mom's superpowers."

Student A wrote: "My mom can cook three dishes, take a phone call, and yell at me about homework all at the same time."

Student B wrote: "My mom can hear me opening a snack bag from five meters away."

Student C wrote: "My mom's superpower is that no matter what time I come home, she's never actually asleep."

The teacher collected the essays, marked halfway through,
then stopped and sent a text to her own mother:

"Mom, Happy Mother's Day. Thanks for having all these superpowers too."

Her mom replied in three words: "Go to sleep."

Some abilities are clearly inherited.
en.editorNote: Perfect for a Mother's Day group chat — everyone will spot their own mom in at least one of those superpowers, and the teacher's ending gives the whole thing an unexpected extra layer
variations: Extended version: "Mom superpowers don't require training. They unlock automatically the moment you become a mom. The instruction manual is you."

items.sourceNote: Labor Day doing-nothing concept from Punsify Labor Day Jokes collection; original scene rewritten as an office worker's internal monologue about resting on May Day

zh-tw.content:
五月一日勞動節。

同事傳訊息：「今天是勞動節，你在幹嘛？」

我：「在休息。」

同事：「對，今天是勞工的節日，要好好慶祝。」

我：「我在認真休息。把休息當成一項任務在完成。」

同事：「……你是不是把躺平也排了行程？」

我：「早上十點到十一點：放空。
十一點到十二點：發呆。
下午兩點到三點：考慮要不要動一下，結論是不要。」

同事沉默了一下：「這比我們的工作計畫書還詳細。」

我：「因為這是我今年最認真執行的計劃。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每個把放假也要排成行程的完美主義打工人——「認真休息」這個矛盾的組合，說中了很多人的假日狀態
variations: 簡版：「勞動節我決定認真休息——因為你連休息都不認真，那你的勞動算什麼？」

en.content:
May 1st. Labor Day.

A coworker texted: "It's Labor Day — what are you up to?"

Me: "Resting."

Coworker: "Right, it's the workers' holiday. Celebrate properly."

Me: "I am resting properly. I'm treating rest as a task I need to complete."

Coworker: "...Did you actually schedule your lying around?"

Me: "10–11am: stare at nothing.
11am–noon: zone out.
2–3pm: consider moving. Conclusion: no."

Coworker paused: "This is more detailed than our actual project plans."

Me: "Because this is the most seriously I've executed any plan this year."
en.editorNote: For any over-scheduled perfectionist who can't even rest without making it a task — 'treating rest as a deliverable' is a completely accurate diagnosis of the modern worker on their day off
variations: Short version: "Labor Day rule: rest seriously. Because if you can't even rest properly, what does that say about the rest of your work?"

items.sourceNote: None of my jokes about the unemployed work — concept from Chuckle Daddy Labor Day collection; original scene rewritten as a stand-up comedian dealing with a heckler at a workers' day show

zh-tw.content:
勞動節晚會，一個喜劇演員上台。

「大家好，我有一些關於失業的笑話——」

台下有人喊：「說來聽聽！」

演員：「但是這些笑話……都不太管用。」

台下安靜了一秒。

「等等，你是在諷刺失業者找不到工作，
還是在說你的笑話本身失業了？」

演員：「兩個都是。」

全場大笑。

散場時有人跟他說：「這是我今年聽過最有效的失業笑話了。」

演員：「謝謝，我練習了很久，但這種笑話在家裡講沒有效果。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給喜歡繞口冷笑話的朋友——笑話本身「失業」這個 meta 結構，讓整個段子有自我指涉的幽默層次，笑點是給懂的人準備的
variations: 單句冷笑話版：「我有一些關於失業的笑話，但它們都不管用。所以我猜我的笑話跟它們的主角一樣，暫時賦閒中。」

en.content:
Labor Day variety show. A stand-up comedian walks on stage.

"Hey everyone — I've got some jokes about unemployment—"

Someone in the crowd: "Let's hear them!"

Comedian: "The thing is... none of them work."

Silence for exactly one second.

"Wait — are you making a joke about unemployed people being out of work,
or are you saying your jokes themselves are unemployed?"

Comedian: "Both."

The crowd lost it.

Afterward someone came up to him: "That's the most effective unemployment joke I've heard all year."

Comedian: "Thanks. I practiced a long time, but it never lands at home."
He paused. "Which, come to think of it, is also pretty on theme."
en.editorNote: For the friend who loves meta-humor and self-referential wordplay — the joke about unemployment jokes being themselves unemployed is the kind of recursive wit that lands hardest with people who already know the format
variations: One-liner version: "I have some jokes about unemployment. They don't work. Which, honestly, makes them the most accurate jokes I've ever told."

items.sourceNote: "Because I said so" classic mom catchphrase from Parade mom jokes; rewritten as a debate-club-style father-daughter scene with the punchline being mom's logic-proof argument

zh-tw.content:
女兒參加辯論社回家，跟媽媽說：「媽，我今天學到，要說服別人，必須要有邏輯、證據、跟反例。」

媽媽：「嗯。」

女兒：「所以我可以晚上跟同學去看電影嗎？」

媽媽：「不行。」

女兒：「為什麼？」

媽媽：「因為我說不行。」

女兒：「這沒有邏輯也沒有證據。」

媽媽：「我就是邏輯，我就是證據，我就是反例。」

女兒愣了三秒。

「……老師沒有教我怎麼破解這個。」

媽媽：「因為這是只有當媽媽才會解鎖的招式。」

母親節快樂，無敵的媽媽。
zh-tw.editorNote: 母親節傳給媽媽群組的萬用段子——「我就是邏輯」這招天下無敵，每個被媽媽用「因為我說不行」KO 過的小孩都會心一笑
variations: 短版：「媽媽不需要邏輯，因為她本人就是邏輯。」

en.content:
My daughter came home from debate club and announced: "Mom, I learned today that to convince someone, you need logic, evidence, and counter-examples."

Mom: "Mm-hm."

Daughter: "So can I go to the movies with friends tonight?"

Mom: "No."

Daughter: "Why?"

Mom: "Because I said so."

Daughter: "That's not logic or evidence."

Mom: "I am the logic. I am the evidence. I am the counter-example."

Daughter froze for three seconds.

"...My teacher didn't cover how to counter this one."

Mom: "That's because it's a move only unlocked by becoming a mother."

Happy Mother's Day to the undefeated.
en.editorNote: A Mother's Day favorite for the family chat — every kid who's ever been KO'd by 'because I said so' will recognize the move. The mom-as-logic-itself escalation is what makes it land
variations: Short version: "Moms don't need logic. Moms ARE logic."

items.sourceNote: Mom-themed pun "mini-mum" / mom puns from Punflip Mother's Day collection; rewritten as an original gift-shopping scene where the punchline is the kid genuinely doesn't understand math anymore

zh-tw.content:
小明母親節要買禮物，跑去問店員：

「請問你們最划算的禮物是哪一個？」

店員指著一個小盒子：「這個，最迷你（mini）的。」

小明：「這太小了吧，我媽會生氣。」

店員：「但這叫 mini-mum，給 mum 的最佳選擇。」

小明沉默了。

「我可以給她最大的嗎？」

店員：「那叫 maxi-mum，給最捨得花錢的小孩。」

小明結帳的時候默默選了中間那個。

店員：「這個是 medi-mum——給數學還沒放棄的小孩。」

小明：「我數學早就放棄了，我只是錢不夠。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給愛雙關語的朋友——「mini-mum / maxi-mum / medi-mum」三段遞進，最後小明的吐槽完美打破節日感人氛圍，現實又好笑
variations: 短版：「給 mum 的禮物有三種：mini-mum、medi-mum、maxi-mum。我每年都選 medi-mum，因為我永遠錢只夠中間那個。」

en.content:
Tim went shopping for a Mother's Day gift and asked the clerk:

"What's your best deal?"

Clerk pointed to a tiny box: "This one. The smallest. The mini-mum."

Tim: "That's way too small. My mom will be mad."

Clerk: "But it's literally called mini-mum. Best choice for mum."

Tim went quiet.

"Can I get the biggest one?"

Clerk: "That's the maxi-mum. For kids who don't mind going broke."

Tim ended up picking the one in the middle.

Clerk: "Ah, the medi-mum. For kids who haven't given up on math yet."

Tim: "I gave up on math years ago. I just don't have enough cash."
en.editorNote: For the friend who lives for puns — the mini/medi/maxi-mum escalation hits harder because Tim's brutally honest punchline grounds the whole joke in real-life broke-kid energy
variations: Short version: "Mother's Day gifts come in three sizes: mini-mum, medi-mum, maxi-mum. I always pick medi-mum, because that's all my budget will mum-ster."

items.sourceNote: Mom's coffee dependency theme from Callie collection; rewritten as a fresh kitchen scene where the punchline is mom's coffee being a sentient family member

zh-tw.content:
母親節早上，我端了一杯咖啡到媽媽床邊。

「媽，母親節快樂。」

媽媽眼睛沒睜開，伸手摸到杯子，喝了一口。

停頓三秒。

「這是即溶咖啡。」

我：「對。」

媽媽：「我用了二十年的時間，把你從一個只會哭的小嬰兒，養成一個會說話、會走路、會考試的人類。」

我：「對。」

媽媽：「然後你今天，端一杯即溶咖啡給我。」

我：「我有加糖。」

媽媽閉上眼睛：「這是我這輩子最失敗的投資。」

但她還是把整杯喝完了。

因為媽媽真正需要的，從來不是咖啡的等級——是有人記得她。
zh-tw.editorNote: 笑點＋催淚的雙效組合，適合傳到媽媽群組——前面靠即溶咖啡的吐槽逗笑，最後一句反轉直接擊中親情要害
variations: 短版：「媽媽喝咖啡不挑等級，只挑誰泡的。是你泡的，再難喝她也喝完。」

en.content:
Mother's Day morning. I brought Mom a cup of coffee in bed.

"Happy Mother's Day, Mom."

Eyes still closed, she reached for the cup and took a sip.

Three-second pause.

"This is instant coffee."

Me: "Yes."

Mom: "I spent twenty years turning you from a crying lump into a human being who can walk, talk, and pass exams."

Me: "Yes."

Mom: "And today, you bring me instant coffee."

Me: "I added sugar."

Mom closed her eyes: "This is the worst return on investment of my entire life."

She drank the whole cup anyway.

Because what moms actually need isn't premium coffee — it's someone who remembered.
en.editorNote: A laugh-then-cry combo for the family group chat — the instant-coffee insult lands hard, but the final line yanks the rug out and turns it into something that actually means something
variations: Short version: "Moms don't care what brand of coffee you bring them. They care that you brought it."

items.sourceNote: "Where do eggs go on vacation — New Yolk City" pun-style summer joke from Scout Life collection; rewritten as a parent-kid car trip with original escalating wordplay

zh-tw.content:
暑假開車出門旅遊，七歲的兒子在後座問：

「爸爸，雞蛋會去哪裡度假？」

爸爸：「不知道，去哪？」

兒子：「紐蛋（New Yolk）。」

媽媽笑出聲。

爸爸：「那鯊魚去哪？」

兒子：「鯊國（Finland，fin = 鰭）。」

爸爸：「機器人呢？」

兒子：「充電島（recharge）。」

爸爸：「最後一題——爸爸去哪？」

兒子認真想了五秒：

「⋯⋯銀行。」

爸爸沉默了。

媽媽：「答對了，獎品是這趟旅遊的所有費用。」

爸爸看了一下油表，悲傷地嘆了一口氣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 暑假家庭旅遊段子，傳給帶過小孩出去玩的爸媽——前面諧音梗鋪墊，最後爸爸去銀行的punchline把每個帶小孩出遊的家長都打中
variations: 簡版：「暑假旅遊的真相是：小孩去玩，媽媽去煩，爸爸去銀行。」

en.content:
Summer road trip. From the back seat, my seven-year-old asks:

"Dad, where do eggs go on vacation?"

Dad: "I don't know, where?"

Kid: "New Yolk City."

Mom snorts.

Dad: "Where do sharks go?"

Kid: "Finland."

Dad: "What about robots?"

Kid: "Recharge Island."

Dad: "Last one — where does Dad go?"

Kid thinks seriously for five seconds.

"...The bank."

Dad goes silent.

Mom: "Correct. Prize: this entire trip."

Dad checks the gas gauge and sighs the sigh of a man who knows.
en.editorNote: A summer road-trip joke for any parent who's footed the bill for a 'fun' family vacation — the kid-pun setup is just camouflage for the dad-goes-to-the-bank gut punch at the end
variations: Short version: "Summer vacation rule: the kids go to the beach, mom goes nuts, and dad goes to the bank."

items.sourceNote: "Why is Mother's Day before Father's Day — Mom needs a day off before she has to plan his" classic format from One Line Fun; rewritten as an LINE conversation between two moms in a group chat

zh-tw.content:
媽媽群組訊息。

小美媽：「為什麼母親節在父親節前面？」

阿芳媽：「不知道，為什麼？」

小美媽：「因為媽媽需要先休一天，才有力氣去策劃爸爸的那一天。」

群組安靜了五秒。

阿芳媽：「我笑了，但我老公看到會生氣。」

佩佩媽：「他不會看到的，他連母親節是哪天都不知道。」

小美媽：「重點是——母親節的禮物、餐廳、卡片，是誰買的？」

阿芳媽：「我自己。」

佩佩媽：「我自己。」

小美媽：「我也是。」

三個媽媽同時傳了一張流淚的貼圖。

然後又繼續討論晚餐要煮什麼。
zh-tw.editorNote: 母親節傳到媽媽群組會引發大共鳴的段子——靠媽媽們自己幫自己慶祝的真實感打中所有當媽的人，最後一句「繼續煮晚餐」把笑點拉到無奈又溫柔的層次
variations: 單句版：「母親節的禮物，通常是媽媽自己買、自己包、自己拆——順便還要假裝驚喜。」

en.content:
Mom group chat:

Mom A: "Why is Mother's Day before Father's Day?"

Mom B: "Why?"

Mom A: "Because moms need a day off first, so we have the energy to plan his."

Five seconds of silence.

Mom B: "I laughed. But my husband would not."

Mom C: "He won't see it. He doesn't even know what day Mother's Day is."

Mom A: "Real question — who's buying the gift, booking the restaurant, and writing the card this year?"

Mom B: "Me."

Mom C: "Me."

Mom A: "Same."

Three crying-laughing emojis appear simultaneously.

Then they go right back to figuring out what to cook for dinner.
en.editorNote: A Mother's Day group-chat joke that lands hardest with moms who already know — the punchline isn't the setup, it's the moms-buying-their-own-gifts confession and the seamless return to dinner planning
variations: One-liner: "On Mother's Day, the gift is usually one Mom bought, wrapped, and unwrapped herself — plus the Oscar-worthy surprise face."

items.sourceNote: "Labor Day motto: work hard, nap harder" rest-themed pun from Punsify Labor Day collection; rewritten as a Taiwanese office worker's 5/1 morning showdown with his alarm clock

zh-tw.content:
五月一號早上六點，鬧鐘響了。

阿凱伸手按掉，腦子裡開始辯論：

身體：「今天勞動節。」

良心：「對。」

身體：「勞動節是慶祝勞工的節日。」

良心：「對。」

身體：「那最能慶祝勞工的方式是什麼？」

良心：「⋯⋯不勞動？」

身體：「答對了。」

阿凱翻身，繼續睡。

睡到中午，老婆走進房間：

「你不是說要早起去爬山慶祝勞動節嗎？」

阿凱閉著眼睛：「我正在用最忠於勞工本質的方式慶祝。」

老婆：「⋯⋯什麼意思？」

阿凱：「努力工作的反義詞，就是放心地睡。」

老婆默默把窗簾拉上，順便也躺下了。

勞動節快樂，全國最忠誠的勞工。
zh-tw.editorNote: 勞動節傳給上班族朋友的萬用段子——把「慶祝勞工」翻譯成「徹底放空」的歪理邏輯，配上老婆最後一起躺平的轉折，整個段子精準命中所有被工作搾乾的人
variations: 短版：「勞動節最忠於精神的慶祝方式，就是徹底地、不懷愧疚地——睡到自然醒。」

en.content:
6 AM, May 1st. The alarm goes off.

Kai slaps it down. A debate begins inside his head.

Body: "Today is Labor Day."

Conscience: "Correct."

Body: "Labor Day celebrates workers."

Conscience: "Correct."

Body: "So what's the most authentic way to celebrate workers?"

Conscience: "...Not laboring?"

Body: "Exactly."

Kai rolls over and goes back to sleep.

Noon. His wife walks in.

"Weren't you supposed to go hiking to celebrate Labor Day?"

Kai, eyes still closed: "I'm celebrating in the way most faithful to the soul of the worker."

Wife: "...Meaning?"

Kai: "The opposite of working hard is napping harder."

She quietly closes the curtains and lies down too.

Happy Labor Day from the nation's most loyal worker.
en.editorNote: A Labor Day joke for the friend who treats the holiday as a sacred duty to nap — the wife joining in at the end is what turns it from grumble into solidarity. Send it to your most exhausted coworker on May 1
variations: Short version: "Labor Day motto: work hard, nap harder. The truest worker is the one who's earned the right to do absolutely nothing."

items.sourceNote: "I used to own a paper company, but it folded" / "I always wanted to be a barber but couldn't cut it" failed-job pun lineup from Chuckle Daddy; rewritten as a Labor Day reunion with three failed-career friends one-upping each other

zh-tw.content:
勞動節聚餐，三個老朋友在比誰的職業失敗最有梗。

阿明：「我以前開紙廠，後來倒了——是真的『摺』了。」

阿華：「還好。我以前學理髮，最後發現我這個人——剪不下去。」

阿傑想了三秒：「我以前在電梯公司，後來離職了——因為這份工作起起伏伏，我受不了。」

全場沉默。

服務生剛好走過來：「請問三位點什麼？」

三個人同時：「再來一輪啤酒，我們的職業生涯需要一點液體支援。」

服務生：「我以前是調酒師，後來轉行——因為工作太『調』了。」

阿明：「⋯⋯你被錄取了，今天的酒我請。」

勞動節快樂，獻給所有努力過、失敗過、然後還能笑出來的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 勞動節傳給職場好友群組的段子——四個人輪流接諧音哏，最後服務生神來一筆無縫加入，完美捕捉「中年人聚會自嘲職涯」的精髓
variations: 短版：「我以前開紙廠，後來摺了。我以前學理髮，剪不下去。我以前在電梯公司，起起伏伏受不了。所以現在——我什麼都不做，最穩定。」

en.content:
Labor Day reunion dinner. Three old friends competing over whose career flop has the best pun.

Ming: "I used to own a paper company. It folded."

Hua: "Not bad. I trained as a barber. Turned out I just... couldn't cut it."

Jay thought for three seconds: "I worked at an elevator company. I quit — too many ups and downs for me."

Dead silence.

The waiter happens to walk over: "Ready to order?"

All three: "Another round of beers. Our careers need liquid support."

Waiter: "I used to be a bartender. Had to leave — the work was too stirring."

Ming: "...You're hired. Drinks are on me tonight."

Happy Labor Day to everyone who's tried, failed, and still gets to laugh about it.
en.editorNote: A Labor Day pun chain for the work-friends group chat — four people stacking failed-career jokes, with the waiter as the surprise MVP. Captures the exact energy of mid-career friends roasting their own resumes
variations: Short version: "My paper company folded, my barber career didn't cut it, my elevator job had too many ups and downs. So now I do nothing — most stable gig I've ever had."

items.sourceNote: "I asked my mom what she wanted — she said nothing would make me happier than a clean house, so I got her exactly that: nothing" classic format from Parade; rewritten as a sibling text-thread debate over what counts as a real Mother's Day gift

zh-tw.content:
母親節前一週，兄妹三人的群組爆炸。

大姊：「今年送媽什麼？」

二哥：「我問過了，她說『不用花錢，把家裡打掃乾淨我就最開心』。」

小妹：「太好了，那我們什麼都不用買。」

大姊：「⋯⋯你認真的？」

小妹：「她自己說的，我只是按照她的需求精準執行。」

二哥：「但她那句話翻譯成中文是『我希望有人主動幫我，而且還要送我禮物』。」

小妹：「翻譯來源是哪？」

二哥：「三十年的母子相處經驗。」

大姊：「所以結論是——我們要打掃家裡『而且』還要買禮物？」

二哥：「對。」

小妹：「那她當初幹嘛說『不用花錢』？」

二哥：「那是測試題，答對的小孩會被列入遺囑。」

群組瞬間安靜，三個人開始下單禮物。
zh-tw.editorNote: 母親節傳到兄弟姐妹群組必爆笑的段子——「媽媽說不用花錢」這個經典陷阱題，每個亞洲家庭的小孩都被考過，二哥的「列入遺囑」punchline 直接把整個群組炸醒
variations: 短版：「媽媽說『不用送禮物』的時候，正確答案不是『不送』，是『送，而且要驚喜』。這題沒有標準答案，但有標準下場。」

en.content:
Week before Mother's Day. The sibling group chat explodes.

Oldest sister: "What are we getting Mom this year?"

Middle brother: "Already asked. She said 'don't spend money, just clean the house and I'll be happy.'"

Youngest: "Perfect. We buy nothing."

Oldest: "...Are you serious?"

Youngest: "She literally said it. I'm just executing her stated requirements."

Brother: "In Mom-language, that translates to: 'I want someone to take initiative AND get me a gift.'"

Youngest: "Source on that translation?"

Brother: "Thirty years of being her son."

Oldest: "So the conclusion is — clean the house AND buy a gift?"

Brother: "Correct."

Youngest: "Then why did she SAY 'don't spend money'?"

Brother: "It's a test. Kids who answer correctly get added to the will."

Group chat goes silent. All three start adding gifts to cart.
en.editorNote: A Mother's Day sibling-chat joke that detonates in every family group — every adult child has failed the 'don't get me anything' trap question. The 'added to the will' punchline ends the debate instantly
variations: Short version: "When Mom says 'don't get me anything,' the right answer isn't 'okay.' It's 'okay AND here's a gift AND I cleaned the house.' This is a graded test."

items.sourceNote: original composition

zh-tw.content:
母親節當天，兒子捧著一束康乃馨回家。

媽媽：「哎喲，這束花多少錢？」

兒子：「八百八。」

媽媽：「⋯⋯你瘋了？這種花我去市場買兩百就有一大把。」

兒子：「但這是母親節限定，比較貴。」

媽媽：「那你下禮拜再買嘛，一定打折。」

兒子：「下禮拜就不是母親節了。」

媽媽：「下禮拜你就還是我兒子啊。」

兒子愣在原地。

三秒後，他默默拿出手機。

媽媽：「你幹嘛？」

兒子：「我把下禮拜的晚餐也訂了，這樣就划算了。」

媽媽嘴角藏不住笑：「⋯⋯訂貴一點的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 母親節經典「媽媽嫌貴但又開心」的場景——亞洲媽媽的口是心非寫到極致，最後一句「訂貴一點的」是整個段子的靈魂，傳到媽媽群組必有共鳴
variations: 短版：「媽媽說花太貴的時候，她不是在嫌花，她是在提醒你——下禮拜還是要記得回家吃飯。」

en.content:
Mother's Day. The son walks in with a bouquet of carnations.

Mom: "How much was that?"

Son: "Eighty-eight bucks."

Mom: "...Have you lost your mind? I can get the same flowers for twenty at the market."

Son: "It's the Mother's Day premium."

Mom: "Then buy them next week. Guaranteed discount."

Son: "Next week isn't Mother's Day."

Mom: "Next week you'll still be my son."

The son freezes.

Three seconds later, he quietly picks up his phone.

Mom: "What are you doing?"

Son: "Booking next week's dinner too. Now it's a bulk deal."

Mom can't hide her smile: "...Book the expensive place."

Happy Mother's Day to every mom who complains about the price and still keeps the receipt.
en.editorNote: The classic Asian-mom move — scolds you for spending money, then quietly upgrades the order. The 'book the expensive place' button is the heart of the joke. Send it to the family chat the night before Mother's Day
variations: Short version: "When Mom says the flowers were too expensive, she's not complaining about the flowers. She's reminding you to come home for dinner next week too."

items.sourceNote: original composition

zh-tw.content:
端午節前一天，公司群組丟出問題：

主管：「中午訂粽子，請大家投票，甜的還是鹹的？」

阿明：「鹹的。」

小芳：「甜的。」

阿傑：「都可以。」

小妹：「我吃肉粽。」

阿志：「那不就是鹹的？」

小妹：「不一樣，肉粽是肉粽，鹹粽是鹹粽。」

阿明：「⋯⋯你哪裡人？」

小妹：「南部。」

全南部同事：「+1」

全北部同事：「？？？」

主管沉默了三十秒，最後打出：「我自己包，誰都別吵。」

隔天阿嬤級的水煮粽端上桌。

沒有人敢再投票。
zh-tw.editorNote: 端午節辦公室群組必爆梗——南北粽之爭是台灣每年端午節的固定節目，主管最後一句「我自己包」直接終結戰爭，傳到同事群組秒被洗版
variations: 短版：「南北粽不能比，比的不是味道，是各自的童年。比贏了沒朋友，比輸了沒家。」

en.content:
Day before Dragon Boat Festival. The office chat lights up.

Manager: "Ordering zongzi for lunch. Vote: sweet or savory?"

Ming: "Savory."

Fang: "Sweet."

Jay: "I'm flexible."

Mei: "I want meat zongzi."

Zhi: "...That's just savory."

Mei: "No it's not. Meat zongzi is meat zongzi. Savory zongzi is savory zongzi."

Ming: "...Where are you from?"

Mei: "Southern Taiwan."

Every southerner: "+1"

Every northerner: "???"

The manager goes quiet for thirty seconds, then types: "I'll wrap them myself. Nobody argue."

Next day, grandma-tier boiled zongzi shows up on the table.

Nobody dares to vote anymore.

Happy Dragon Boat Festival from your nation's most divided lunch order.
en.editorNote: The eternal North vs South zongzi war, restaged in an office chat. Every Taiwanese workplace has lived this exact thread — the manager's 'I'll wrap them myself' is the only known peace treaty
variations: Short version: "Sweet zongzi vs savory zongzi isn't a debate about flavor. It's a debate about whose childhood was real. Nobody wins."

items.sourceNote: original composition

zh-tw.content:
中秋節晚上，全家在頂樓烤肉。

爸爸負責烤，烤了二十分鐘，所有東西都還是生的。

小孩：「爸，我餓了。」

爸爸：「再等一下，火還沒到位。」

媽媽：「你那個火從你大學就沒到位過。」

爸爸：「⋯⋯妳這樣講話，這片肉就更不熟了。」

媽媽：「你這個人，烤肉烤到連物理學都被你重新定義。」

阿公從旁邊走過，默默拿走夾子。

五分鐘後，肉香四溢，全家圍過來吃。

爸爸假裝若無其事：「我剛剛只是先幫你們鋪墊味道。」

阿公：「鋪墊三十年了，鋪墊得真久。」

中秋節快樂，獻給所有負責烤肉但不一定會烤的爸爸們。
zh-tw.editorNote: 中秋節烤肉場景必中梗——爸爸自信滿滿但烤不熟、媽媽神補刀、阿公默默接手，三代同堂的經典畫面，傳到家族群組保證大姑姑小阿姨同時按讚
variations: 短版：「中秋節爸爸的烤肉哲學：火候到了不一定熟，但一定要看起來很有信心。」

en.content:
Mid-Autumn Festival night. The whole family is grilling on the rooftop.

Dad's in charge. Twenty minutes in, everything is still raw.

Kid: "Dad, I'm starving."

Dad: "Hold on. The fire isn't ready yet."

Mom: "That fire hasn't been ready since college."

Dad: "...You keep talking like that, this meat's gonna take even longer."

Mom: "You've personally redefined the laws of physics with this grill."

Grandpa walks by and silently takes the tongs.

Five minutes later, the smell of actually-cooked meat fills the air. Family swarms in.

Dad, pretending nothing happened: "I was just laying down the flavor base for him."

Grandpa: "You've been laying that base for thirty years."

Happy Mid-Autumn Festival to every dad who confidently runs the grill without actually knowing how.
en.editorNote: The universal Mid-Autumn grill scene — confident dad, sharp mom, silent rescue grandpa. Every multigenerational family chat will recognize at least one of these characters. The 'redefined physics' line lands hardest with anyone who's eaten dad's cooking
variations: Short version: "Dad's Mid-Autumn grilling philosophy: the meat doesn't have to be cooked, but you have to look extremely confident about it."

items.sourceNote: 母親節「想要什麼禮物→什麼都不用」classic gag from 母親節祝福語 collections; rewritten with original dialogue and modern setting

zh-tw.content:
母親節前一週。

我：「媽，妳想要什麼禮物？」

媽：「我什麼都不需要，你健健康康就好。」

我：「真的不用？」

媽：「真的不用。」

我：「那我就不買了。」

媽：「⋯⋯」

媽：「你這個小孩是不是哪裡有問題。」

我：「妳剛剛不是說——」

媽：「我說的是場面話，你應該要懂。」

我：「那妳到底想要什麼？」

媽：「你猜啊，猜不到就是不夠用心。」

我打開購物網站。

滑了三個小時。

最後買了一束花、一張卡片、一頓晚餐，外加一罐她每次經過專櫃都會看一眼但從來不買的精華液。

媽收到禮物，假裝生氣：「叫你不要花錢，你都不聽。」

但是嘴角上揚了。

母親節快樂，獻給所有說不要其實要、嘴硬心軟的媽媽們。
zh-tw.editorNote: 母親節最寫實的母女／母子對話——「我什麼都不需要」是場面話，「你猜啊」才是真心話。傳到家族群組或當作母親節貼文文案，所有當過小孩的人都會點頭
variations: 短版：「媽說『我什麼都不要』的真正意思是『你最好給我猜對』，這是身為小孩的基本閱讀能力。」

en.content:
One week before Mother's Day.

Me: "Mom, what do you want for a gift?"

Mom: "I don't need anything. As long as you're healthy, I'm happy."

Me: "Really? Nothing?"

Mom: "Really. Nothing."

Me: "Okay then I won't buy anything."

Mom: "..."

Mom: "What is wrong with you as a child."

Me: "You literally just said—"

Mom: "That was the polite version. You're supposed to read between the lines."

Me: "So what do you actually want?"

Mom: "Guess. If you can't guess, that means you don't care enough."

I open the shopping app.

Scroll for three hours.

End up with flowers, a card, dinner reservations, and that one serum she stares at every time she walks past the counter but never buys.

She receives the gift, pretending to be annoyed: "I told you not to spend money. You never listen."

But her mouth is twitching upward.

Happy Mother's Day to every mom who says she wants nothing and means everything.
en.editorNote: The most universally accurate Mother's Day exchange ever written. 'Nothing' never means nothing. 'Guess' is the real test. Works as a Mother's Day caption that every adult child will share with the comment 'this is literally my mom'
variations: Short version: "When Mom says 'I don't want anything,' she means 'you'd better guess correctly.' This is basic comprehension for being someone's child."

items.sourceNote: 「因為我是媽媽」=undefeated argument trope, common in Mother's Day humor lists; rewritten as original conversation

zh-tw.content:
小時候，我曾經試著跟媽媽講道理。

我：「為什麼不行？」

媽：「因為不行。」

我：「但是這樣不合邏輯。」

媽：「我是媽媽，我就是邏輯。」

我：「這不是辯論，這是獨裁。」

媽：「對，恭喜你聽懂了。」

那一年我七歲。

二十年後，我自己當了媽，第一次對小孩說出「因為我是媽媽」這句話的時候。

我打給我媽。

我：「媽，我懂了。」

媽：「妳終於懂了。」

我：「這句話真的很好用。」

媽：「妳以為我們為什麼要堅持把它傳下去？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 「因為我是媽媽」是全世界共通的終極論點——適合傳給剛當媽的朋友，或母親節寫給媽媽的訊息。最後一句「我們為什麼要堅持把它傳下去」是世代相傳的笑點
variations: 短版：「跟媽媽辯論的下場只有兩種：你輸，或者你還沒輸但快輸了。」

en.content:
When I was little, I tried to argue with my mom.

Me: "Why not?"

Mom: "Because not."

Me: "But that's not logical."

Mom: "I'm the mom. I am logic."

Me: "This isn't a debate. This is a dictatorship."

Mom: "Correct. Glad you're keeping up."

I was seven.

Twenty years later, when I became a mom myself and said "because I'm the mom" out loud for the first time —

I called her.

Me: "Mom. I get it now."

Mom: "There it is."

Me: "That phrase is incredibly effective."

Mom: "Why do you think we kept passing it down?"

Happy Mother's Day to the original undefeated debate champion of your life.
en.editorNote: 'Because I'm the mom' — the universally accepted nuclear option in any household argument. Hits hardest for new parents who finally understand. The 'why do you think we kept passing it down' line is the generational punchline
variations: Short version: "There are only two outcomes when arguing with Mom: you lose, or you haven't lost yet."

items.sourceNote: 「媽媽找東西的超能力」trope — common in Mother's Day humor; rewritten as original household scenario

zh-tw.content:
我：「媽，我的鑰匙呢？」

媽：「在你房間。」

我：「我找過了，沒有。」

媽：「在你書桌左邊抽屜，第二層，前面那本筆記本下面。」

我走回房間，打開抽屜，第二層，掀開筆記本。

鑰匙就在那裡。

我走出來。

我：「媽，妳怎麼知道？」

媽：「我什麼都知道。」

我：「妳上次走進我房間是去年三月。」

媽：「對。」

我：「那妳——」

媽：「我說了，我什麼都知道。」

我安靜地走回房間。

母親節快樂，獻給所有擁有「找東西雷達」的媽媽，妳們的監視範圍比 Google 街景還要全面。
zh-tw.editorNote: 媽媽的「找東西雷達」是亞洲家庭萬年金牌段子——明明妳完全沒注意，但妳什麼都知道。適合配上凌亂房間的照片或當作母親節感謝文案
variations: 短版：「媽媽不需要監視器，她有更可怕的東西——她的記憶力。」

en.content:
Me: "Mom, where are my keys?"

Mom: "In your room."

Me: "I checked. They're not there."

Mom: "Left side desk drawer, second one down, under the notebook in front."

I walk back. Open the drawer. Second one down. Lift the notebook.

Keys.

I walk back out.

Me: "Mom, how did you know?"

Mom: "I know everything."

Me: "You haven't been in my room since March of last year."

Mom: "Correct."

Me: "So how—"

Mom: "I said. I know everything."

I quietly walk back to my room.

Happy Mother's Day to every mom whose lost-item radar covers more square footage than Google Street View.

We don't know how you do it. We've stopped asking.
en.editorNote: Mom's lost-item radar is the universal family superpower. She hasn't been in your room in months but she knows exactly which drawer, which layer, which notebook. Hits everyone who has ever been a child looking for keys
variations: Short version: "Moms don't need surveillance cameras. They have something scarier — perfect memory of where you put things you forgot you owned."

items.sourceNote: 母親節送禮被嫌貴 trope from Taiwanese 母親節梗圖 archives; rewritten as original family scene

zh-tw.content:
母親節，我送了媽媽一個包包。

媽：「多少錢？」

我：「不貴啦。」

媽：「多少錢？」

我：「真的不貴。」

媽：「你不講我會生氣。」

我：「⋯⋯三千多。」

媽：「三千多？！你瘋了嗎？！這個錢可以買多少菜！」

媽繼續念了三分鐘。

念完之後，她拿著包包進房間。

十分鐘後出來，已經換上新洋裝、化好妝、揹著新包包。

媽：「我去市場一下。」

我：「⋯⋯妳剛剛不是說太貴？」

媽：「貴歸貴，菜還是要買。」

我看著她揹著三千多的包，去買一斤五十塊的青菜。

母親節快樂，獻給所有口嫌體正直的媽媽，妳們的演技值得一座金鐘獎。
zh-tw.editorNote: 母親節送禮經典場景——媽媽嘴上嫌貴心裡爽，揹著新包去買青菜是台灣媽媽的招牌畫面。傳到家族群組會被各種「我媽也是」洗版
variations: 短版：「媽媽嫌禮物貴的時間有多久，揹出去炫耀的時間就有多久。」

en.content:
Mother's Day. I gave Mom a handbag.

Mom: "How much?"

Me: "It wasn't expensive."

Mom: "How much."

Me: "Really, it wasn't."

Mom: "If you don't tell me I'm going to be upset."

Me: "...About a hundred bucks."

Mom: "A HUNDRED?! Are you insane?! Do you know how many groceries that buys?!"

She lectures for three minutes.

Then she takes the bag into her room.

Ten minutes later she emerges in a new dress, full makeup, the bag over her shoulder.

Mom: "I'm running to the market real quick."

Me: "...Didn't you just say it was too expensive?"

Mom: "Expensive is expensive. Groceries are groceries."

I watch her walk out carrying a hundred-dollar bag to buy two dollars of vegetables.

Happy Mother's Day to every mom whose mouth says 'too much' and whose shoulder says 'show this thing off everywhere.'
en.editorNote: The classic Mother's Day gift scene — Mom complains about the price for three minutes, then immediately takes the bag out to the market. Lands instantly with anyone whose mom has ever 'reluctantly' worn their gift to the grocery store
variations: Short version: "The amount of time Mom complains about a gift's price is exactly equal to the amount of time she'll show it off."

items.sourceNote: 「打電話只回兩句」 mom phone trope from English Mother's Day one-liners; rewritten as original Taiwanese family scene

zh-tw.content:
母親節打電話回家。

我：「媽，母親節快樂！」

媽：「喔，謝謝。」

我：「妳今天吃飯了嗎？」

媽：「有啊。」

我：「吃什麼？」

媽：「便當。」

我：「最近身體怎麼樣？」

媽：「還好。」

我：「⋯⋯」

我：「妳是不是在忙？」

媽：「沒有啊。」

我：「那妳怎麼都只回兩個字？」

媽：「我等你講重點。」

我：「我的重點就是想你。」

媽（停頓三秒）：「⋯⋯肉麻死了。」

媽（再停頓三秒）：「我也是。」

然後她迅速掛掉電話。

母親節快樂，獻給所有嘴硬到爆但其實偷偷錄音的媽媽。
zh-tw.editorNote: 媽媽電話只回兩個字、卻在「我也是」之後秒掛——是亞洲媽媽表達愛的標準格式。傳到家族群組或當母親節限時動態文案，所有打過電話給媽媽的人都會被擊中
variations: 短版：「媽媽說『我也是』之後三秒掛電話，是她這輩子最浪漫的瞬間。」

en.content:
Mother's Day phone call.

Me: "Mom! Happy Mother's Day!"

Mom: "Oh. Thanks."
